(P9721) == * 高部屋神社.jpg {{commons category|Category:Takabeya-jinja}} [[JA:高部屋神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11672973}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = 高部屋神社 |画像 = [[File:高部屋神社.jpg|250px]]
鳥居と拝殿 |所在地 = {{jalink|神奈川県|Q127513}}{{jalink|伊勢原市|Q734520}}下糟屋2202 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = 神倭伊波礼彦命・誉田別命・三筒男命・大鷦鷯命・息気長足姫命・磐之姫命 |社格 = {{jalink|式内社}}（小）、{{jalink|村社|Q134917284}}（{{jalink|近代社格制度|Q712534|旧社格}}） |創建 = 不詳 |本殿 = |別名 = 八幡神社（糟屋八幡） |例祭 = {{jalink|9月15日|Q2846}} |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Kanagawa|label=高部屋神社|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''高部屋神社'''（たかべやじんじゃ）は{{jalink|神奈川県|Q127513}}{{jalink|伊勢原市|Q734520}}下糟屋にある{{jalink|神社|Q845945}}。{{jalink|延長 (元号)|Q1327274|延長}}5年（{{jalink|927年|Q30470}}）の『{{jalink|延喜式神名帳|Q11064932}}』に記載されている{{jalink|相模国|Q1053732}}の{{jalink|相模国の式内社一覧|Q11582137|延喜式内社十三社}}の内の一社（小社）とされる{{sfn|新編相模国風土記稿 下糟屋村 八幡宮}}が、後述のように{{jalink|論社|Q135022904}}も存在する。{{jalink|近代社格制度|Q712534|旧社格}}は村社。 === 概要 === かつてはこの神社と同名の{{jalink|大住郡|Q11432367}}{{jalink|高部屋村|Q11672972}}の村社であり、またこの地区を含む{{jalink|糟屋庄}}糟屋庄の糟屋の地名は現在も残っているが、「上'''粕'''屋」と「下'''糟'''屋」で字が違うため注意が必要である。の{{jalink|総鎮守}}としても崇敬された。 創建年代は不詳。『{{jalink|延喜式神名帳|Q11064932}}』に相模国13座の内の1社と記載されており、大住郡127ヶ村の惣鎮守であった。ただし、伊勢原市高森にある{{jalink|高森神社 (伊勢原市)||高森神社}}も、式内社「高部屋神社」の論社の一つとされており、この神社が{{jalink|明治|Q3125096}}の{{jalink|神仏分離令}}以前は「高部屋神社」という呼び名であったと伝えられていることに加え、棟木や{{jalink|鳥居|Q234731}}脇の{{jalink|石碑|Q178743}}（裏側）に「高部屋神社」という銘文が残されていることもその理由として挙げられる[http://w1.oroti.net/~takamori/htdocs/ 相模の国 高森神社]（公式ホームページ）の由緒のページ参照。 {{jalink|鎌倉時代|Q236205}}に、糟屋庄の{{jalink|地頭|Q1329805}}であった{{jalink|源頼朝|Q242800}}の家人{{jalink|糟屋有季|Q11604765|糟屋藤太左兵衛尉有季}}が、高部屋神社を守護神として新たな{{jalink|社殿}}を造営した。{{jalink|至徳 (日本)|Q1146202|至徳}}3年（{{jalink|1386年|Q6355}}）12月に河内守宗国によって造られ、平秀憲が寄進した銅鐘は{{jalink|神奈川県指定文化財一覧|Q11588977|神奈川県指定重要文化財}}に指定されている。{{jalink|天文 (元号)|Q1192853|天文}}20年（{{jalink|1551年|Q6560}}）に地頭の渡辺石見守が社殿を再興した{{sfn|新編相模国風土記稿 下糟屋村 八幡宮}}。{{jalink|天正|Q1145058}}19年（{{jalink|1591年|Q6766}}）11月に{{jalink|徳川家康|Q171977}}より社領10石の御朱印を賜る{{sfn|新編相模国風土記稿 下糟屋村 八幡宮}}。{{jalink|江戸時代|Q184963}}中期頃まで別名「糟屋八幡宮」と呼ばれた。 また、{{jalink|正保|Q1146192}}4年（{{jalink|1647年|Q6935}}）に本殿を再建されるが、{{jalink|関東大震災|Q3742341}}で倒壊し、{{jalink|昭和|Q327567}}4年（{{jalink|1929年|Q18792}}）に柱・彫刻・正面扉等をそのまま使用し再建される。{{jalink|慶応|Q776775}}元年（{{jalink|1865年|Q7708}}）には拝殿を再建。 神事に用いられる鎌倉時代以降の雅楽面三面の古面や、{{jalink|京都府|Q120730}}{{jalink|宇治市|Q326956}}にある黄檗山{{jalink|萬福寺|Q576318}}7世・悦山道宗（{{jalink|中国|Q29520}}{{jalink|福建省|Q41705}}泉州府晋江県からの渡来僧）が{{jalink|元禄|Q1325001}}初期に揮毫した「八幡宮」の扁額がある。本殿前の狛犬を寄進した行按・行白も{{jalink|黄檗宗|Q384728}}の僧で、一時期、別当神宮寺とともに黄檗宗との関係が深かったと思われる。 現在は{{jalink|伊勢原大神宮|Q11378955}}が管理を兼務しており、{{jalink|朱印 (神社仏閣)|Q7504772|朱印}}等はそちらでもらうことができる。 === 歴史 === * {{jalink|1551年|Q6560}}（天文20年） - 当地の{{jalink|地頭|Q1329805}}渡辺石見守によって社殿が再建される。 * {{jalink|1591年|Q6766}}（天正19年） - 徳川家康によって朱印10石が寄進される。 * {{jalink|1865年|Q7708}}（慶応元年） - 社殿が再興する。 * {{jalink|1923年|Q2169}}（大正12年） - {{jalink|関東大震災|Q3742341}}によって本殿が倒壊。 * {{jalink|1929年|Q18792}}（昭和4年） - 本殿が再建される。 === 神事 === [[File:Takabeya-jinja(Isehara) Kaguraden.jpg|200px|right|thumb|神楽殿]] {{jalink|平安時代|Q193292}}より一千年以上続く神事として「汐汲みの神事」が9月に執り行われる。儀式に使われる浜砂は、{{jalink|住吉大神}}（大住大明神）が上陸したと言われる照ヶ崎海岸から採取する。{{jalink|明治|Q3125096}}初期頃までは照ヶ崎まで片道16{{jalink|キロメートル|Q828224|km}}の道のりを宮当番と神主が歩き、海水と浜砂、海藻のホンダワラ（馬尾藻）を採取していた。海水は鎮火水とし、浜砂はお清めのために参道に撒き、ホンダワラは拝殿と鳥居の注連縄に吊るす。 === 境内 === 境内には本殿・拝殿、{{jalink|神楽殿|Q94498}}、御祓所、{{jalink|手水舎}}、{{jalink|梵鐘|Q8192099}}の他に、{{jalink|遊具|Q7111945}}や以下の{{jalink|境内社}}がある。 === 国の登録有形文化財 === ; 本殿 : 関東大震災で倒壊したが、昭和４年に再建された。主要な木材は、{{jalink|正保|Q1146192}}４年（1647年）再建した倒壊前の本殿を再利用している。なお、平成28年2月25日に国の登録有形文化財に登録されている。 ; 拝殿 : 慶応元年（1865年）に再建された。なお、本殿と同様に平成28年2月25日に国の登録有形文化財に登録されている。 === 神奈川県の重要文化財 === ; 鐘楼 : {{jalink|至徳 (日本)|Q1146202|至徳}}3年（1386年）12月に平秀憲が銅鐘を寄進した。なお、銅鐘は神奈川県の重要文化財に指定されている。 File:Takabeya-jinja(Isehara) Haiden.jpg|拝殿 File:Takabeya-jinja(Isehara) Honden.jpg|本殿 File:Takabeya-jinja(Isehara) Shourou.jpg|鐘楼 === 境内社 === * {{jalink|八坂神社 (曖昧さ回避)|Q2599651|八坂神社}} * {{jalink|金刀比羅神社|Q11646488|金刀比羅宮}} * {{jalink|稲荷社}}、{{jalink|水神社 (曖昧さ回避)||水神社}}、{{jalink|庚申社|Q11486003}}、{{jalink|愛染明王|Q1188900}} File:Takabeya-jinja(Isehara) Inarisha.jpg|稲荷社 File:Takabeya-jinja(Isehara) Mizugami.jpg|水神 === 拝殿の画像 === ファイル:Tesshu takabeya 00.jpg|拝殿正面軒下には、{{jalink|山岡鉄舟|Q1158894}}の筆による「髙部屋神社」の社号額が掲げられている。 ファイル:Takabeya_honden.jpg|拝殿向拝の彫刻 === 交通 === * {{jalink|小田急電鉄|Q83584|小田急}}{{jalink|小田急小田原線|Q135176|小田原線}}{{jalink|伊勢原駅|Q4205219}}より徒歩20分ほど。 * 同駅北口より{{jalink|神奈川中央交通|Q10854960}}バス ** {{jalink|神奈川中央交通伊勢原営業所#伊勢原駅北口 - 愛甲石田駅方面|Q11588882|伊76系統}}「{{jalink|愛甲石田駅|Q1107009}}」行きに乗車、「粕屋上宿」バス停下車、徒歩3分。 ** {{jalink|神奈川中央交通伊勢原営業所#伊勢原駅北口 - 愛甲石田駅方面|Q11588882|伊74系統}}「愛甲石田駅」行きに乗車、「粟窪入口」バス停下車、徒歩4分。 (P9721) == * 高部屋神社.jpg {{commons category|Category:Takabeya-jinja}} [[JA:高部屋神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11672973}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = 高部屋神社 |画像 = [[File:高部屋神社.jpg|250px]]
鳥居と拝殿 |所在地 = {{jalink|神奈川県|Q127513}}{{jalink|伊勢原市|Q734520}}下糟屋2202 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = 神倭伊波礼彦命・誉田別命・三筒男命・大鷦鷯命・息気長足姫命・磐之姫命 |社格 = {{jalink|式内社}}（小）、{{jalink|村社|Q134917284}}（{{jalink|近代社格制度|Q712534|旧社格}}） |創建 = 不詳 |本殿 = |別名 = 八幡神社（糟屋八幡） |例祭 = {{jalink|9月15日|Q2846}} |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Kanagawa|label=高部屋神社|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''高部屋神社'''（たかべやじんじゃ）は{{jalink|神奈川県|Q127513}}{{jalink|伊勢原市|Q734520}}下糟屋にある{{jalink|神社|Q845945}}。{{jalink|延長 (元号)|Q1327274|延長}}5年（{{jalink|927年|Q30470}}）の『{{jalink|延喜式神名帳|Q11064932}}』に記載されている{{jalink|相模国|Q1053732}}の{{jalink|相模国の式内社一覧|Q11582137|延喜式内社十三社}}の内の一社（小社）とされる{{sfn|新編相模国風土記稿 下糟屋村 八幡宮}}が、後述のように{{jalink|論社|Q135022904}}も存在する。{{jalink|近代社格制度|Q712534|旧社格}}は村社。 === 概要 === かつてはこの神社と同名の{{jalink|大住郡|Q11432367}}{{jalink|高部屋村|Q11672972}}の村社であり、またこの地区を含む{{jalink|糟屋庄}}糟屋庄の糟屋の地名は現在も残っているが、「上'''粕'''屋」と「下'''糟'''屋」で字が違うため注意が必要である。の{{jalink|総鎮守}}としても崇敬された。 創建年代は不詳。『{{jalink|延喜式神名帳|Q11064932}}』に相模国13座の内の1社と記載されており、大住郡127ヶ村の惣鎮守であった。ただし、伊勢原市高森にある{{jalink|高森神社 (伊勢原市)||高森神社}}も、式内社「高部屋神社」の論社の一つとされており、この神社が{{jalink|明治|Q3125096}}の{{jalink|神仏分離令}}以前は「高部屋神社」という呼び名であったと伝えられていることに加え、棟木や{{jalink|鳥居|Q234731}}脇の{{jalink|石碑|Q178743}}（裏側）に「高部屋神社」という銘文が残されていることもその理由として挙げられる[http://w1.oroti.net/~takamori/htdocs/ 相模の国 高森神社]（公式ホームページ）の由緒のページ参照。 {{jalink|鎌倉時代|Q236205}}に、糟屋庄の{{jalink|地頭|Q1329805}}であった{{jalink|源頼朝|Q242800}}の家人{{jalink|糟屋有季|Q11604765|糟屋藤太左兵衛尉有季}}が、高部屋神社を守護神として新たな{{jalink|社殿}}を造営した。{{jalink|至徳 (日本)|Q1146202|至徳}}3年（{{jalink|1386年|Q6355}}）12月に河内守宗国によって造られ、平秀憲が寄進した銅鐘は{{jalink|神奈川県指定文化財一覧|Q11588977|神奈川県指定重要文化財}}に指定されている。{{jalink|天文 (元号)|Q1192853|天文}}20年（{{jalink|1551年|Q6560}}）に地頭の渡辺石見守が社殿を再興した{{sfn|新編相模国風土記稿 下糟屋村 八幡宮}}。{{jalink|天正|Q1145058}}19年（{{jalink|1591年|Q6766}}）11月に{{jalink|徳川家康|Q171977}}より社領10石の御朱印を賜る{{sfn|新編相模国風土記稿 下糟屋村 八幡宮}}。{{jalink|江戸時代|Q184963}}中期頃まで別名「糟屋八幡宮」と呼ばれた。 また、{{jalink|正保|Q1146192}}4年（{{jalink|1647年|Q6935}}）に本殿を再建されるが、{{jalink|関東大震災|Q3742341}}で倒壊し、{{jalink|昭和|Q327567}}4年（{{jalink|1929年|Q18792}}）に柱・彫刻・正面扉等をそのまま使用し再建される。{{jalink|慶応|Q776775}}元年（{{jalink|1865年|Q7708}}）には拝殿を再建。 神事に用いられる鎌倉時代以降の雅楽面三面の古面や、{{jalink|京都府|Q120730}}{{jalink|宇治市|Q326956}}にある黄檗山{{jalink|萬福寺|Q576318}}7世・悦山道宗（{{jalink|中国|Q29520}}{{jalink|福建省|Q41705}}泉州府晋江県からの渡来僧）が{{jalink|元禄|Q1325001}}初期に揮毫した「八幡宮」の扁額がある。本殿前の狛犬を寄進した行按・行白も{{jalink|黄檗宗|Q384728}}の僧で、一時期、別当神宮寺とともに黄檗宗との関係が深かったと思われる。 現在は{{jalink|伊勢原大神宮|Q11378955}}が管理を兼務しており、{{jalink|朱印 (神社仏閣)|Q7504772|朱印}}等はそちらでもらうことができる。 === 歴史 === * {{jalink|1551年|Q6560}}（天文20年） - 当地の{{jalink|地頭|Q1329805}}渡辺石見守によって社殿が再建される。 * {{jalink|1591年|Q6766}}（天正19年） - 徳川家康によって朱印10石が寄進される。 * {{jalink|1865年|Q7708}}（慶応元年） - 社殿が再興する。 * {{jalink|1923年|Q2169}}（大正12年） - {{jalink|関東大震災|Q3742341}}によって本殿が倒壊。 * {{jalink|1929年|Q18792}}（昭和4年） - 本殿が再建される。 === 神事 === [[File:Takabeya-jinja(Isehara) Kaguraden.jpg|200px|right|thumb|神楽殿]] {{jalink|平安時代|Q193292}}より一千年以上続く神事として「汐汲みの神事」が9月に執り行われる。儀式に使われる浜砂は、{{jalink|住吉大神}}（大住大明神）が上陸したと言われる照ヶ崎海岸から採取する。{{jalink|明治|Q3125096}}初期頃までは照ヶ崎まで片道16{{jalink|キロメートル|Q828224|km}}の道のりを宮当番と神主が歩き、海水と浜砂、海藻のホンダワラ（馬尾藻）を採取していた。海水は鎮火水とし、浜砂はお清めのために参道に撒き、ホンダワラは拝殿と鳥居の注連縄に吊るす。 === 境内 === 境内には本殿・拝殿、{{jalink|神楽殿|Q94498}}、御祓所、{{jalink|手水舎}}、{{jalink|梵鐘|Q8192099}}の他に、{{jalink|遊具|Q7111945}}や以下の{{jalink|境内社}}がある。 === 国の登録有形文化財 === ; 本殿 : 関東大震災で倒壊したが、昭和４年に再建された。主要な木材は、{{jalink|正保|Q1146192}}４年（1647年）再建した倒壊前の本殿を再利用している。なお、平成28年2月25日に国の登録有形文化財に登録されている。 ; 拝殿 : 慶応元年（1865年）に再建された。なお、本殿と同様に平成28年2月25日に国の登録有形文化財に登録されている。 === 神奈川県の重要文化財 === ; 鐘楼 : {{jalink|至徳 (日本)|Q1146202|至徳}}3年（1386年）12月に平秀憲が銅鐘を寄進した。なお、銅鐘は神奈川県の重要文化財に指定されている。 File:Takabeya-jinja(Isehara) Haiden.jpg|拝殿 File:Takabeya-jinja(Isehara) Honden.jpg|本殿 File:Takabeya-jinja(Isehara) Shourou.jpg|鐘楼 === 境内社 === * {{jalink|八坂神社 (曖昧さ回避)|Q2599651|八坂神社}} * {{jalink|金刀比羅神社|Q11646488|金刀比羅宮}} * {{jalink|稲荷社}}、{{jalink|水神社 (曖昧さ回避)||水神社}}、{{jalink|庚申社|Q11486003}}、{{jalink|愛染明王|Q1188900}} ファイル:Takabeya_honden.jpg|拝殿向拝の彫刻 === 交通 === * {{jalink|小田急電鉄|Q83584|小田急}}{{jalink|小田急小田原線|Q135176|小田原線}}{{jalink|伊勢原駅|Q4205219}}より徒歩20分ほど。 * 同駅北口より{{jalink|神奈川中央交通|Q10854960}}バス ** (P9721) == * 高部屋神社.jpg {{commons category|Category:Takabeya-jinja}} [[JA:高部屋神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11672973}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = 高部屋神社 |画像 = [[File:高部屋神社.jpg|250px]]
鳥居と拝殿 |所在地 = {{jalink|神奈川県|Q127513}}{{jalink|伊勢原市|Q734520}}下糟屋2202 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = 神倭伊波礼彦命・誉田別命・三筒男命・大鷦鷯命・息気長足姫命・磐之姫命 |社格 = {{jalink|式内社}}（小）、{{jalink|村社|Q134917284}}（{{jalink|近代社格制度|Q712534|旧社格}}） |創建 = 不詳 |本殿 = |別名 = 八幡神社（糟屋八幡） |例祭 = {{jalink|9月15日|Q2846}} |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Kanagawa|label=高部屋神社|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''高部屋神社'''（たかべやじんじゃ）は{{jalink|神奈川県|Q127513}}{{jalink|伊勢原市|Q734520}}下糟屋にある{{jalink|神社|Q845945}}。{{jalink|延長 (元号)|Q1327274|延長}}5年（{{jalink|927年|Q30470}}）の『{{jalink|延喜式神名帳|Q11064932}}』に記載されている{{jalink|相模国|Q1053732}}の{{jalink|相模国の式内社一覧|Q11582137|延喜式内社十三社}}の内の一社（小社）とされる{{sfn|新編相模国風土記稿 下糟屋村 八幡宮}}が、後述のように{{jalink|論社|Q135022904}}も存在する。{{jalink|近代社格制度|Q712534|旧社格}}は村社。 === 概要 === かつてはこの神社と同名の{{jalink|大住郡|Q11432367}}{{jalink|高部屋村|Q11672972}}の村社であり、またこの地区を含む{{jalink|糟屋庄}}糟屋庄の糟屋の地名は現在も残っているが、「上'''粕'''屋」と「下'''糟'''屋」で字が違うため注意が必要である。の{{jalink|総鎮守}}としても崇敬された。 創建年代は不詳。『{{jalink|延喜式神名帳|Q11064932}}』に相模国13座の内の1社と記載されており、大住郡127ヶ村の惣鎮守であった。ただし、伊勢原市高森にある{{jalink|高森神社 (伊勢原市)||高森神社}}も、式内社「高部屋神社」の論社の一つとされており、この神社が{{jalink|明治|Q3125096}}の{{jalink|神仏分離令}}以前は「高部屋神社」という呼び名であったと伝えられていることに加え、棟木や{{jalink|鳥居|Q234731}}脇の{{jalink|石碑|Q178743}}（裏側）に「高部屋神社」という銘文が残されていることもその理由として挙げられる[http://w1.oroti.net/~takamori/htdocs/ 相模の国 高森神社]（公式ホームページ）の由緒のページ参照。 {{jalink|鎌倉時代|Q236205}}に、糟屋庄の{{jalink|地頭|Q1329805}}であった{{jalink|源頼朝|Q242800}}の家人{{jalink|糟屋有季|Q11604765|糟屋藤太左兵衛尉有季}}が、高部屋神社を守護神として新たな{{jalink|社殿}}を造営した。{{jalink|至徳 (日本)|Q1146202|至徳}}3年（{{jalink|1386年|Q6355}}）12月に河内守宗国によって造られ、平秀憲が寄進した銅鐘は{{jalink|神奈川県指定文化財一覧|Q11588977|神奈川県指定重要文化財}}に指定されている。{{jalink|天文 (元号)|Q1192853|天文}}20年（{{jalink|1551年|Q6560}}）に地頭の渡辺石見守が社殿を再興した{{sfn|新編相模国風土記稿 下糟屋村 八幡宮}}。{{jalink|天正|Q1145058}}19年（{{jalink|1591年|Q6766}}）11月に{{jalink|徳川家康|Q171977}}より社領10石の御朱印を賜る{{sfn|新編相模国風土記稿 下糟屋村 八幡宮}}。{{jalink|江戸時代|Q184963}}中期頃まで別名「糟屋八幡宮」と呼ばれた。 また、{{jalink|正保|Q1146192}}4年（{{jalink|1647年|Q6935}}）に本殿を再建されるが、{{jalink|関東大震災|Q3742341}}で倒壊し、{{jalink|昭和|Q327567}}4年（{{jalink|1929年|Q18792}}）に柱・彫刻・正面扉等をそのまま使用し再建される。{{jalink|慶応|Q776775}}元年（{{jalink|1865年|Q7708}}）には拝殿を再建。 神事に用いられる鎌倉時代以降の雅楽面三面の古面や、{{jalink|京都府|Q120730}}{{jalink|宇治市|Q326956}}にある黄檗山{{jalink|萬福寺|Q576318}}7世・悦山道宗（{{jalink|中国|Q29520}}{{jalink|福建省|Q41705}}泉州府晋江県からの渡来僧）が{{jalink|元禄|Q1325001}}初期に揮毫した「八幡宮」の扁額がある。本殿前の狛犬を寄進した行按・行白も{{jalink|黄檗宗|Q384728}}の僧で、一時期、別当神宮寺とともに黄檗宗との関係が深かったと思われる。 現在は{{jalink|伊勢原大神宮|Q11378955}}が管理を兼務しており、{{jalink|朱印 (神社仏閣)|Q7504772|朱印}}等はそちらでもらうことができる。 === 歴史 === * {{jalink|1551年|Q6560}}（天文20年） - 当地の{{jalink|地頭|Q1329805}}渡辺石見守によって社殿が再建される。 * {{jalink|1591年|Q6766}}（天正19年） - 徳川家康によって朱印10石が寄進される。 * {{jalink|1865年|Q7708}}（慶応元年） - 社殿が再興する。 * {{jalink|1923年|Q2169}}（大正12年） - {{jalink|関東大震災|Q3742341}}によって本殿が倒壊。 * {{jalink|1929年|Q18792}}（昭和4年） - 本殿が再建される。 === 神事 === [[File:Takabeya-jinja(Isehara) Kaguraden.jpg|200px|right|thumb|神楽殿]] {{jalink|平安時代|Q193292}}より一千年以上続く神事として「汐汲みの神事」が9月に執り行われる。儀式に使われる浜砂は、{{jalink|住吉大神}}（大住大明神）が上陸したと言われる照ヶ崎海岸から採取する。{{jalink|明治|Q3125096}}初期頃までは照ヶ崎まで片道16{{jalink|キロメートル|Q828224|km}}の道のりを宮当番と神主が歩き、海水と浜砂、海藻のホンダワラ（馬尾藻）を採取していた。海水は鎮火水とし、浜砂はお清めのために参道に撒き、ホンダワラは拝殿と鳥居の注連縄に吊るす。 === 境内 === 境内には本殿・拝殿、{{jalink|神楽殿|Q94498}}、御祓所、{{jalink|手水舎}}、{{jalink|梵鐘|Q8192099}}の他に、{{jalink|遊具|Q7111945}}や以下の{{jalink|境内社}}がある。 === 国の登録有形文化財 === ; 本殿 : 関東大震災で倒壊したが、昭和４年に再建された。主要な木材は、{{jalink|正保|Q1146192}}４年（1647年）再建した倒壊前の本殿を再利用している。なお、平成28年2月25日に国の登録有形文化財に登録されている。 ; 拝殿 : 慶応元年（1865年）に再建された。なお、本殿と同様に平成28年2月25日に国の登録有形文化財に登録されている。 === 神奈川県の重要文化財 === ; 鐘楼 : {{jalink|至徳 (日本)|Q1146202|至徳}}3年（1386年）12月に平秀憲が銅鐘を寄進した。なお、銅鐘は神奈川県の重要文化財に指定されている。 File:Takabeya-jinja(Isehara) Haiden.jpg|拝殿 File:Takabeya-jinja(Isehara) Honden.jpg|本殿 File:Takabeya-jinja(Isehara) Shourou.jpg|鐘楼 === 境内社 === * {{jalink|八坂神社 (曖昧さ回避)|Q2599651|八坂神社}} * {{jalink|金刀比羅神社|Q11646488|金刀比羅宮}} * {{jalink|稲荷社}}、{{jalink|水神社 (曖昧さ回避)||水神社}}、{{jalink|庚申社|Q11486003}}、{{jalink|愛染明王|Q1188900}} ファイル:Takabeya_honden.jpg|拝殿向拝の彫刻 === 交通 === * {{jalink|小田急電鉄|Q83584|小田急}}{{jalink|小田急小田原線|Q135176|小田原線}}{{jalink|伊勢原駅|Q4205219}}より徒歩20分ほど。 * 同駅北口より{{jalink|神奈川中央交通|Q10854960}}バス ** (P9721) == * 高部屋神社.jpg {{commons category|Category:Takabeya-jinja}} [[JA:高部屋神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11672973}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = 高部屋神社 |画像 = [[File:高部屋神社.jpg|250px]]
鳥居と拝殿 |所在地 = {{jalink|神奈川県|Q127513}}{{jalink|伊勢原市|Q734520}}下糟屋2202 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = 神倭伊波礼彦命・誉田別命・三筒男命・大鷦鷯命・息気長足姫命・磐之姫命 |社格 = {{jalink|式内社}}（小）、{{jalink|村社|Q134917284}}（{{jalink|近代社格制度|Q712534|旧社格}}） |創建 = 不詳 |本殿 = |別名 = 八幡神社（糟屋八幡） |例祭 = {{jalink|9月15日|Q2846}} |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Kanagawa|label=高部屋神社|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''高部屋神社'''（たかべやじんじゃ）は{{jalink|神奈川県|Q127513}}{{jalink|伊勢原市|Q734520}}下糟屋にある{{jalink|神社|Q845945}}。{{jalink|延長 (元号)|Q1327274|延長}}5年（{{jalink|927年|Q30470}}）の『{{jalink|延喜式神名帳|Q11064932}}』に記載されている{{jalink|相模国|Q1053732}}の{{jalink|相模国の式内社一覧|Q11582137|延喜式内社十三社}}の内の一社（小社）とされる{{sfn|新編相模国風土記稿 下糟屋村 八幡宮}}が、後述のように{{jalink|論社|Q135022904}}も存在する。{{jalink|近代社格制度|Q712534|旧社格}}は村社。 === 概要 === かつてはこの神社と同名の{{jalink|大住郡|Q11432367}}{{jalink|高部屋村|Q11672972}}の村社であり、またこの地区を含む{{jalink|糟屋庄}}糟屋庄の糟屋の地名は現在も残っているが、「上'''粕'''屋」と「下'''糟'''屋」で字が違うため注意が必要である。の{{jalink|総鎮守}}としても崇敬された。 創建年代は不詳。『{{jalink|延喜式神名帳|Q11064932}}』に相模国13座の内の1社と記載されており、大住郡127ヶ村の惣鎮守であった。ただし、伊勢原市高森にある{{jalink|高森神社 (伊勢原市)||高森神社}}も、式内社「高部屋神社」の論社の一つとされており、この神社が{{jalink|明治|Q3125096}}の{{jalink|神仏分離令}}以前は「高部屋神社」という呼び名であったと伝えられていることに加え、棟木や{{jalink|鳥居|Q234731}}脇の{{jalink|石碑|Q178743}}（裏側）に「高部屋神社」という銘文が残されていることもその理由として挙げられる[http://w1.oroti.net/~takamori/htdocs/ 相模の国 高森神社]（公式ホームページ）の由緒のページ参照。 {{jalink|鎌倉時代|Q236205}}に、糟屋庄の{{jalink|地頭|Q1329805}}であった{{jalink|源頼朝|Q242800}}の家人{{jalink|糟屋有季|Q11604765|糟屋藤太左兵衛尉有季}}が、高部屋神社を守護神として新たな{{jalink|社殿}}を造営した。{{jalink|至徳 (日本)|Q1146202|至徳}}3年（{{jalink|1386年|Q6355}}）12月に河内守宗国によって造られ、平秀憲が寄進した銅鐘は{{jalink|神奈川県指定文化財一覧|Q11588977|神奈川県指定重要文化財}}に指定されている。{{jalink|天文 (元号)|Q1192853|天文}}20年（{{jalink|1551年|Q6560}}）に地頭の渡辺石見守が社殿を再興した{{sfn|新編相模国風土記稿 下糟屋村 八幡宮}}。{{jalink|天正|Q1145058}}19年（{{jalink|1591年|Q6766}}）11月に{{jalink|徳川家康|Q171977}}より社領10石の御朱印を賜る{{sfn|新編相模国風土記稿 下糟屋村 八幡宮}}。{{jalink|江戸時代|Q184963}}中期頃まで別名「糟屋八幡宮」と呼ばれた。 また、{{jalink|正保|Q1146192}}4年（{{jalink|1647年|Q6935}}）に本殿を再建されるが、{{jalink|関東大震災|Q3742341}}で倒壊し、{{jalink|昭和|Q327567}}4年（{{jalink|1929年|Q18792}}）に柱・彫刻・正面扉等をそのまま使用し再建される。{{jalink|慶応|Q776775}}元年（{{jalink|1865年|Q7708}}）には拝殿を再建。 神事に用いられる鎌倉時代以降の雅楽面三面の古面や、{{jalink|京都府|Q120730}}{{jalink|宇治市|Q326956}}にある黄檗山{{jalink|萬福寺|Q576318}}7世・悦山道宗（{{jalink|中国|Q29520}}{{jalink|福建省|Q41705}}泉州府晋江県からの渡来僧）が{{jalink|元禄|Q1325001}}初期に揮毫した「八幡宮」の扁額がある。本殿前の狛犬を寄進した行按・行白も{{jalink|黄檗宗|Q384728}}の僧で、一時期、別当神宮寺とともに黄檗宗との関係が深かったと思われる。 現在は{{jalink|伊勢原大神宮|Q11378955}}が管理を兼務しており、{{jalink|朱印 (神社仏閣)|Q7504772|朱印}}等はそちらでもらうことができる。 === 歴史 === * {{jalink|1551年|Q6560}}（天文20年） - 当地の{{jalink|地頭|Q1329805}}渡辺石見守によって社殿が再建される。 * {{jalink|1591年|Q6766}}（天正19年） - 徳川家康によって朱印10石が寄進される。 * {{jalink|1865年|Q7708}}（慶応元年） - 社殿が再興する。 * {{jalink|1923年|Q2169}}（大正12年） - {{jalink|関東大震災|Q3742341}}によって本殿が倒壊。 * {{jalink|1929年|Q18792}}（昭和4年） - 本殿が再建される。 === 神事 === [[File:Takabeya-jinja(Isehara) Kaguraden.jpg|200px|right|thumb|神楽殿]] {{jalink|平安時代|Q193292}}より一千年以上続く神事として「汐汲みの神事」が9月に執り行われる。儀式に使われる浜砂は、{{jalink|住吉大神}}（大住大明神）が上陸したと言われる照ヶ崎海岸から採取する。{{jalink|明治|Q3125096}}初期頃までは照ヶ崎まで片道16{{jalink|キロメートル|Q828224|km}}の道のりを宮当番と神主が歩き、海水と浜砂、海藻のホンダワラ（馬尾藻）を採取していた。海水は鎮火水とし、浜砂はお清めのために参道に撒き、ホンダワラは拝殿と鳥居の注連縄に吊るす。 === 境内 === 境内には本殿・拝殿、{{jalink|神楽殿|Q94498}}、御祓所、{{jalink|手水舎}}、{{jalink|梵鐘|Q8192099}}の他に、{{jalink|遊具|Q7111945}}や以下の{{jalink|境内社}}がある。 === 国の登録有形文化財 === ; 本殿 : 関東大震災で倒壊したが、昭和４年に再建された。主要な木材は、{{jalink|正保|Q1146192}}４年（1647年）再建した倒壊前の本殿を再利用している。なお、平成28年2月25日に国の登録有形文化財に登録されている。 ; 拝殿 : 慶応元年（1865年）に再建された。なお、本殿と同様に平成28年2月25日に国の登録有形文化財に登録されている。 === 神奈川県の重要文化財 === ; 鐘楼 : {{jalink|至徳 (日本)|Q1146202|至徳}}3年（1386年）12月に平秀憲が銅鐘を寄進した。なお、銅鐘は神奈川県の重要文化財に指定されている。 File:Takabeya-jinja(Isehara) Haiden.jpg|拝殿 File:Takabeya-jinja(Isehara) Honden.jpg|本殿 File:Takabeya-jinja(Isehara) Shourou.jpg|鐘楼 === 境内社 === * {{jalink|八坂神社 (曖昧さ回避)|Q2599651|八坂神社}} * {{jalink|金刀比羅神社|Q11646488|金刀比羅宮}} * {{jalink|稲荷社}}、{{jalink|水神社 (曖昧さ回避)||水神社}}、{{jalink|庚申社|Q11486003}}、{{jalink|愛染明王|Q1188900}} File:Takabeya-jinja(Isehara) Inarisha.jpg|稲荷社 File:Takabeya-jinja(Isehara) Mizugami.jpg|水神 === 拝殿の画像 === ファイル:Tesshu takabeya 00.jpg|拝殿正面軒下には、{{jalink|山岡鉄舟|Q1158894}}の筆による「髙部屋神社」の社号額が掲げられている。 ファイル:Takabeya_honden.jpg|拝殿向拝の彫刻 === 交通 === * {{jalink|小田急電鉄|Q83584|小田急}}{{jalink|小田急小田原線|Q135176|小田原線}}{{jalink|伊勢原駅|Q4205219}}より徒歩20分ほど。 * 同駅北口より{{jalink|神奈川中央交通|Q10854960}}バス ** {{jalink|神奈川中央交通伊勢原営業所#伊勢原駅北口 - 愛甲石田駅方面|Q11588882|伊76系統}}「{{jalink|愛甲石田駅|Q1107009}}」行きに乗車、「粕屋上宿」バス停下車、徒歩3分。 ** {{jalink|神奈川中央交通伊勢原営業所#伊勢原駅北口 - 愛甲石田駅方面|Q11588882|伊74系統}}「愛甲石田駅」行きに乗車、「粟窪入口」バス停下車、徒歩4分。 === 脚注 === {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 注釈 === === 出典 === === 参考文献 === * {{cite book|和書|title=大日本地誌大系|volume=第37巻{{jalink|新編相模国風土記稿|Q11503942}}2巻之44村里部大住郡巻之3|chapter=糟屋庄 下糟屋村 八幡宮|id={{NDLJP|1179210/192}}|ref={{sfnref|新編相模国風土記稿 下糟屋村 八幡宮}}|publisher=雄山閣|date=1932-8}} === 関連項目 === {{commonscat|Takabeya-jinja}} * {{jalink|相模国の式内社一覧|Q11582137}} * {{jalink|高森神社 (伊勢原市)||高森神社}}（伊勢原市高森） - 式内社「高部屋神社」の論社の一つ。 * {{jalink|高部屋村|Q11672972}} - 直接の関係はないが、かつて存在した村。 === 外部リンク === * [http://www.kanagawa-jinja.or.jp/search_dtl.php4?jid=819&cd=1209262&scd=&npg=0 高部屋神社]（神奈川県神社庁） * [http://takabeyajinja.blog.fc2.com/ 高部屋神社　ふくろう日誌]（氏子運営公式ブログ） * [https://sites.google.com/view/koujinkai-kagumi-isehara/ 伊勢原 髙神會]（高部屋神社 御神輿保存会公式HP） {{Shinto-stub}} (P9721) == * 高部屋神社.jpg {{commons category|Category:Takabeya-jinja}} [[JA:高部屋神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11672973}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Takabeya Shrine |画像 = [[File:高部屋神社.jpg|250px]]
Torii and worship hall |所在地 = {{jalink|Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513}}{{jalink|Isehara|Q734520}} Shimokasuya 2202 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = Kamuyamatoiwarehiko-no-Mikoto, Hondawake-no-Mikoto, Mitsutsuo-no-Mikoto, Ōsasagi-no-Mikoto, Okinagatarashihime-no-Mikoto, Iwanohime-no-Mikoto |社格 = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (minor), {{jalink|Village Shrine|Q134917284}} (former {{jalink|modern shrine rank system|Q712534|shrine rank}}) |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = |別名 = Hachiman Shrine (Kasuya Hachiman) |例祭 = {{jalink|September 15|Q2846}} |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Kanagawa|label=Takabeya Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Takabeya Shrine''' (たかべやじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in Shimokasuya, {{jalink|Isehara|Q734520}}, {{jalink|Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513}}. It is considered one of the 13 Engishiki shrines (minor shrine) in {{jalink|Sagami Province|Q1053732}} listed in the '{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}}' of Enchō 5 ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}){{sfn|新編相模国風土記稿 下糟屋村 八幡宮}}, though as noted below there are also competing claim shrines ({{jalink|ronsha|Q135022904}}). Its former shrine rank was Village Shrine. === Overview === It was formerly the village shrine of {{jalink|Takabeya Village|Q11672972}} in {{jalink|Ōsumi District|Q11432367}} with the same shrine name, and was also revered as the general tutelary shrine of {{jalink|Kasuya-shō}}The place name Kasuya of Kasuya-shō still remains, but note that “Kami'''Kasu'''ya” and “Shimo'''Kasu'''ya” are written with different characters., including this district. Its founding date is unknown. It is listed in the '{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}' as one of 13 seats in Sagami Province and was the general tutelary shrine of 127 villages in Ōsumi District. However, {{jalink|Takamori Shrine (Isehara)|Q0|Takamori Shrine}} in Takamori, Isehara, is also considered one of the claim shrines for the Shikinaisha “Takabeya Shrine.” One reason is that this shrine is said to have been called “Takabeya Shrine” before the Meiji-era {{jalink|separation of Shinto and Buddhism order|Q1970535}}, and inscriptions reading “Takabeya Shrine” remain on timbers and on the back side of the stone monument beside its {{jalink|torii|Q234731}}[http://w1.oroti.net/~takamori/htdocs/ Sagami no Kuni Takamori Shrine] (official website), history page.. In the {{jalink|Kamakura period|Q236205}}, Kasuya Tōta Saemon no Jō Arisue ({{jalink|Kasuya Arisue|Q11604765}}), a retainer of {{jalink|Minamoto no Yoritomo|Q242800}} and local {{jalink|jitō|Q1329805}} of Kasuya-shō, built a new shrine building with Takabeya Shrine as his guardian deity. A bronze bell made in Shitoku 3 ({{jalink|1386|Q6355}}) December by Munekuni, governor of Kawachi, and donated by Taira Hidenori is designated an Important Cultural Property of Kanagawa Prefecture. In Tenbun 20 ({{jalink|1551|Q6560}}), local jitō Watanabe Iwami-no-kami restored the shrine buildings{{sfn|新編相模国風土記稿 下糟屋村 八幡宮}}. In Tenshō 19 ({{jalink|1591|Q6766}}) November, it received a red-seal grant of 10 koku of shrine land from {{jalink|Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977}}{{sfn|新編相模国風土記稿 下糟屋村 八幡宮}}. Until around the mid-Edo period, it was also called “Kasuya Hachimangū.” The main sanctuary was rebuilt in Shōhō 4 ({{jalink|1647|Q6935}}), but collapsed in the {{jalink|Great Kantō Earthquake|Q3742341}}; in Shōwa 4 ({{jalink|1929|Q18792}}) it was rebuilt reusing the columns, carvings, and front doors. The worship hall was rebuilt in Keiō 1 ({{jalink|1865|Q7708}}). The shrine preserves three old gagaku masks used in rituals from the Kamakura period onward, and a plaque reading “Hachimangū” written in early Genroku by Etsuzan Dōsō, the 7th abbot of {{jalink|Manpuku-ji|Q576318}} (Ōbaku-zan, Uji, Kyoto), a monk who came from Jinjiang County, Quanzhou Prefecture, Fujian, China. Gyōan and Gyōhaku, who donated the komainu in front of the main sanctuary, were also monks of the {{jalink|Ōbaku school|Q384728}}, suggesting a period of strong ties with Ōbaku along with the former bettō temple Jingū-ji. At present it is concurrently administered by {{jalink|Isehara Daijingū|Q11378955}}, where one may also receive {{jalink|shuin|Q7504772|seal stamps}}. === History === * {{jalink|1551|Q6560}} (Tenbun 20) - Shrine buildings rebuilt by local {{jalink|jitō|Q1329805}} Watanabe Iwami-no-kami. * {{jalink|1591|Q6766}} (Tenshō 19) - 10-koku red-seal grant donated by Tokugawa Ieyasu. * {{jalink|1865|Q7708}} (Keiō 1) - Shrine buildings restored. * {{jalink|1923|Q2169}} (Taishō 12) - Main sanctuary collapsed in the {{jalink|Great Kantō Earthquake|Q3742341}}. * {{jalink|1929|Q18792}} (Shōwa 4) - Main sanctuary rebuilt. === Rituals === [[File:Takabeya-jinja(Isehara) Kaguraden.jpg|200px|right|thumb|Kagura hall]] A ritual called “Shiokumi no Shinji” has been held in September for over 1,000 years since the {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}}. The beach sand used in the rite is taken from Terugasaki Coast, where {{jalink|Sumiyoshi Ōkami}} (Ōsumi Daimyōjin) is said to have landed. Until around the early {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} period, the shrine duty group and priest walked the 16 {{jalink|kilometer|Q828224|km}} one way to Terugasaki to collect seawater, beach sand, and hondawara seaweed (sargassum). The seawater is used as fire-quelling sacred water; the beach sand is spread on the approach for purification; the hondawara is hung on shimenawa at the worship hall and torii. === Precincts === Within the precincts are the main sanctuary, worship hall, {{jalink|kagura hall|Q94498}}, purification place, {{jalink|temizuya}}, {{jalink|bonshō|Q8192099|temple bell}}, playground equipment, and the following precinct shrines. === National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === ; Main sanctuary : Collapsed in the Great Kantō Earthquake and rebuilt in Shōwa 4. Most major timbers are reused from the pre-collapse main sanctuary rebuilt in {{jalink|Shōhō|Q1146192}} 4 (1647). Registered as a National Registered Tangible Cultural Property on February 25, 2016. ; Worship hall : Rebuilt in Keiō 1 (1865). Like the main sanctuary, registered as a National Registered Tangible Cultural Property on February 25, 2016. === Important Cultural Property of Kanagawa Prefecture === ; Bell tower : In {{jalink|Shitoku|Q1146202|Shitoku}} 3 (1386) December, Taira Hidenori donated a bronze bell. The bell is designated an Important Cultural Property of Kanagawa Prefecture. Mizugami.jpg|Water deity === Images of the worship hall === ファイル:Tesshu takabeya 00.jpg|Under the eaves at the front of the worship hall is a shrine-name plaque “髙部屋神社” written by {{jalink|Yamaoka Tesshū|Q1158894}}. ファイル:Takabeya_honden.jpg|Carving on the worship-hall kōhai === Access === * About 20 minutes on foot from {{jalink|Isehara Station|Q4205219}} on the {{jalink|Odakyu Odawara Line|Q135176}}. * Bus from the station north exit on {{jalink|Kanagawa Chūō Kōtsū|Q10854960}}: ** {{jalink|I76 route|Q11588882}} bound for {{jalink|Aiko-Ishida Station|Q1107009}}, get off at “Kasuya-Kamijuku,” 3-minute walk. ** {{jalink|I74 route|Q11588882}} bound for Aiko-Ishida Station, get off at “Awakubo Iriguchi,” 4-minute walk. === Footnotes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Notes === === References === === Bibliography === * {{cite book|ja|title=Dai Nihon Chishi Taikei|volume=Vol.37 {{jalink|Shinpen Sagami no Kuni Fudokikō|Q11503942}} 2, Village Section, Ōsumi District Vol.3|chapter=Kasuya-shō Shimokasuya Village Hachimangū|id={{NDLJP|1179210/192}}|ref={{sfnref|新編相模国風土記稿 下糟屋村 八幡宮}}|publisher=Yūzankaku|date=1932-8}} === Related items === {{commonscat|Takabeya-jinja}} * {{jalink|List of Shikinaisha in Sagami Province|Q11582137}} * {{jalink|Takamori Shrine (Isehara)|Q0|Takamori Shrine}} (Takamori, Isehara) - one of the claim shrines for the Shikinaisha “Takabeya Shrine.” * {{jalink|Takabeya Village|Q11672972}} - a former village; not directly related. === External links === * [http://www.kanagawa-jinja.or.jp/search_dtl.php4?jid=819&cd=1209262&scd=&npg=0 (P9721) == * 高部屋神社.jpg {{commons category|Category:Takabeya-jinja}} [[JA:高部屋神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11672973}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Takabeya Shrine |画像 = [[File:高部屋神社.jpg|250px]]
Torii and worship hall |所在地 = {{jalink|Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513}}{{jalink|Isehara|Q734520}} Shimokasuya 2202 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = Kamuyamatoiwarehiko-no-Mikoto, Hondawake-no-Mikoto, Mitsutsuo-no-Mikoto, Ōsasagi-no-Mikoto, Okinagatarashihime-no-Mikoto, Iwanohime-no-Mikoto |社格 = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (minor), {{jalink|Village Shrine|Q134917284}} (former {{jalink|modern shrine rank system|Q712534|shrine rank}}) |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = |別名 = Hachiman Shrine (Kasuya Hachiman) |例祭 = {{jalink|September 15|Q2846}} |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Kanagawa|label=Takabeya Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Takabeya Shrine''' (たかべやじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in Shimokasuya, {{jalink|Isehara|Q734520}}, {{jalink|Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513}}. It is considered one of the 13 Engishiki shrines (minor shrine) in {{jalink|Sagami Province|Q1053732}} listed in the '{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}}' of Enchō 5 ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}){{sfn|新編相模国風土記稿 下糟屋村 八幡宮}}, though as noted below there are also competing claim shrines ({{jalink|ronsha|Q135022904}}). Its former shrine rank was Village Shrine. === Overview === It was formerly the village shrine of {{jalink|Takabeya Village|Q11672972}} in {{jalink|Ōsumi District|Q11432367}} with the same shrine name, and was also revered as the general tutelary shrine of {{jalink|Kasuya-shō}}The place name Kasuya of Kasuya-shō still remains, but note that “Kami'''Kasu'''ya” and “Shimo'''Kasu'''ya” are written with different characters., including this district. Its founding date is unknown. It is listed in the '{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}' as one of 13 seats in Sagami Province and was the general tutelary shrine of 127 villages in Ōsumi District. However, {{jalink|Takamori Shrine (Isehara)|Q0|Takamori Shrine}} in Takamori, Isehara, is also considered one of the claim shrines for the Shikinaisha “Takabeya Shrine.” One reason is that this shrine is said to have been called “Takabeya Shrine” before the Meiji-era {{jalink|separation of Shinto and Buddhism order|Q1970535}}, and inscriptions reading “Takabeya Shrine” remain on timbers and on the back side of the stone monument beside its {{jalink|torii|Q234731}}[http://w1.oroti.net/~takamori/htdocs/ Sagami no Kuni Takamori Shrine] (official website), history page.. In the {{jalink|Kamakura period|Q236205}}, Kasuya Tōta Saemon no Jō Arisue ({{jalink|Kasuya Arisue|Q11604765}}), a retainer of {{jalink|Minamoto no Yoritomo|Q242800}} and local {{jalink|jitō|Q1329805}} of Kasuya-shō, built a new shrine building with Takabeya Shrine as his guardian deity. A bronze bell made in Shitoku 3 ({{jalink|1386|Q6355}}) December by Munekuni, governor of Kawachi, and donated by Taira Hidenori is designated an Important Cultural Property of Kanagawa Prefecture. In Tenbun 20 ({{jalink|1551|Q6560}}), local jitō Watanabe Iwami-no-kami restored the shrine buildings{{sfn|新編相模国風土記稿 下糟屋村 八幡宮}}. In Tenshō 19 ({{jalink|1591|Q6766}}) November, it received a red-seal grant of 10 koku of shrine land from {{jalink|Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977}}{{sfn|新編相模国風土記稿 下糟屋村 八幡宮}}. Until around the mid-Edo period, it was also called “Kasuya Hachimangū.” The main sanctuary was rebuilt in Shōhō 4 ({{jalink|1647|Q6935}}), but collapsed in the {{jalink|Great Kantō Earthquake|Q3742341}}; in Shōwa 4 ({{jalink|1929|Q18792}}) it was rebuilt reusing the columns, carvings, and front doors. The worship hall was rebuilt in Keiō 1 ({{jalink|1865|Q7708}}). The shrine preserves three old gagaku masks used in rituals from the Kamakura period onward, and a plaque reading “Hachimangū” written in early Genroku by Etsuzan Dōsō, the 7th abbot of {{jalink|Manpuku-ji|Q576318}} (Ōbaku-zan, Uji, Kyoto), a monk who came from Jinjiang County, Quanzhou Prefecture, Fujian, China. Gyōan and Gyōhaku, who donated the komainu in front of the main sanctuary, were also monks of the {{jalink|Ōbaku school|Q384728}}, suggesting a period of strong ties with Ōbaku along with the former bettō temple Jingū-ji. At present it is concurrently administered by {{jalink|Isehara Daijingū|Q11378955}}, where one may also receive {{jalink|shuin|Q7504772|seal stamps}}. === History === * {{jalink|1551|Q6560}} (Tenbun 20) - Shrine buildings rebuilt by local {{jalink|jitō|Q1329805}} Watanabe Iwami-no-kami. * {{jalink|1591|Q6766}} (Tenshō 19) - 10-koku red-seal grant donated by Tokugawa Ieyasu. * {{jalink|1865|Q7708}} (Keiō 1) - Shrine buildings restored. * {{jalink|1923|Q2169}} (Taishō 12) - Main sanctuary collapsed in the {{jalink|Great Kantō Earthquake|Q3742341}}. * {{jalink|1929|Q18792}} (Shōwa 4) - Main sanctuary rebuilt. === Rituals === [[File:Takabeya-jinja(Isehara) Kaguraden.jpg|200px|right|thumb|Kagura hall]] A ritual called “Shiokumi no Shinji” has been held in September for over 1,000 years since the {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}}. The beach sand used in the rite is taken from Terugasaki Coast, where {{jalink|Sumiyoshi Ōkami}} (Ōsumi Daimyōjin) is said to have landed. Until around the early {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} period, the shrine duty group and priest walked the 16 {{jalink|kilometer|Q828224|km}} one way to Terugasaki to collect seawater, beach sand, and hondawara seaweed (sargassum). The seawater is used as fire-quelling sacred water; the beach sand is spread on the approach for purification; the hondawara is hung on shimenawa at the worship hall and torii. === Precincts === Within the precincts are the main sanctuary, worship hall, {{jalink|kagura hall|Q94498}}, purification place, {{jalink|temizuya}}, {{jalink|bonshō|Q8192099|temple bell}}, playground equipment, and the following precinct shrines. === National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === ; Main sanctuary : Collapsed in the Great Kantō Earthquake and rebuilt in Shōwa 4. Most major timbers are reused from the pre-collapse main sanctuary rebuilt in {{jalink|Shōhō|Q1146192}} 4 (1647). Registered as a National Registered Tangible Cultural Property on February 25, 2016. ; Worship hall : Rebuilt in Keiō 1 (1865). Like the main sanctuary, registered as a National Registered Tangible Cultural Property on February 25, 2016. === Important Cultural Property of Kanagawa Prefecture === ; Bell tower : In {{jalink|Shitoku|Q1146202|Shitoku}} 3 (1386) December, Taira Hidenori donated a bronze bell. The bell is designated an Important Cultural Property of Kanagawa Prefecture. {{jalink|Kōshin Shrine|Q11486003}}, {{jalink|Aizen Myōō|Q1188900}} File:Takabeya-jinja(Isehara) Yasaka-jinja.jpg|Yasaka Shrine File:Takabeya-jinja(Isehara) Konpiragu.jpg|Konpira Shrine File:Takabeya-jinja(Isehara) Aisme-myouou.jpg|Aizen Myōō File:Takabeya-jinja(Isehara) Koushinsha.jpg|Kōshin Shrine File:Takabeya-jinja(Isehara) Inarisha.jpg|Inari Shrine File:Takabeya-jinja(Isehara) Mizugami.jpg|Water deity === Images of the worship hall === ファイル:Tesshu takabeya 00.jpg|Under the eaves at the front of the worship hall is a shrine-name plaque “髙部屋神社” written by {{jalink|Yamaoka Tesshū|Q1158894}}. ファイル:Takabeya_honden.jpg|Carving on the worship-hall kōhai === Access === * About 20 minutes on foot from {{jalink|Isehara Station|Q4205219}} on the {{jalink|Odakyu Odawara Line|Q135176}}. * Bus from the station north exit on {{jalink|Kanagawa Chūō Kōtsū|Q10854960}}: ** {{jalink|I76 route|Q11588882}} bound for {{jalink|Aiko-Ishida Station|Q1107009}}, get off at “Kasuya-Kamijuku,” 3-minute walk. ** {{jalink|I74 route|Q11588882}} bound for Aiko-Ishida Station, get off at “Awakubo Iriguchi,” 4-minute walk. === Footnotes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Notes === === References === === Bibliography === * {{cite book|ja|title=Dai Nihon Chishi Taikei|volume=Vol.37 {{jalink|Shinpen Sagami no Kuni Fudokikō|Q11503942}} 2, Village Section, Ōsumi District Vol.3|chapter=Kasuya-shō Shimokasuya Village Hachimangū|id={{NDLJP|1179210/192}}|ref={{sfnref|新編相模国風土記稿 下糟屋村 八幡宮}}|publisher=Yūzankaku|date=1932-8}} === Related items === {{commonscat|Takabeya-jinja}} * {{jalink|List of Shikinaisha in Sagami Province|Q11582137}} * {{jalink|Takamori Shrine (Isehara)|Q0|Takamori Shrine}} (Takamori, Isehara) - one of the claim shrines for the Shikinaisha “Takabeya Shrine.” * {{jalink|Takabeya Village|Q11672972}} - a former village; not directly related. === External links === * [http://www.kanagawa-jinja.or.jp/search_dtl.php4?jid=819&cd=1209262&scd=&npg=0 Takabeya Shrine] (Kanagawa Prefecture Shrine Office) * [http://takabeyajinja.blog.fc2.com/ Takabeya Shrine Owl Journal] (official parishioner-run blog) * [https://sites.google.com/view/koujinkai-kagumi-isehara/ Isehara Kōshinkai] (official site of Takabeya Shrine Mikoshi Preservation Association) {{Shinto-stub}} {{Pref-stub|pref=神奈川県}} (P9721) == * 高部屋神社.jpg {{commons category|Category:Takabeya-jinja}} [[JA:高部屋神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11672973}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Takabeya Shrine |画像 = [[File:高部屋神社.jpg|250px]]
Torii and worship hall |所在地 = {{jalink|Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513}}{{jalink|Isehara|Q734520}} Shimokasuya 2202 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = Kamuyamatoiwarehiko-no-Mikoto, Hondawake-no-Mikoto, Mitsutsuo-no-Mikoto, Ōsasagi-no-Mikoto, Okinagatarashihime-no-Mikoto, Iwanohime-no-Mikoto |社格 = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (minor), {{jalink|Village Shrine|Q134917284}} (former {{jalink|modern shrine rank system|Q712534|shrine rank}}) |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = |別名 = Hachiman Shrine (Kasuya Hachiman) |例祭 = {{jalink|September 15|Q2846}} |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Kanagawa|label=Takabeya Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Takabeya Shrine''' (たかべやじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in Shimokasuya, {{jalink|Isehara|Q734520}}, {{jalink|Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513}}. It is considered one of the 13 Engishiki shrines (minor shrine) in {{jalink|Sagami Province|Q1053732}} listed in the '{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}}' of Enchō 5 ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}){{sfn|新編相模国風土記稿 下糟屋村 八幡宮}}, though as noted below there are also competing claim shrines ({{jalink|ronsha|Q135022904}}). Its former shrine rank was Village Shrine. === Overview === It was formerly the village shrine of {{jalink|Takabeya Village|Q11672972}} in {{jalink|Ōsumi District|Q11432367}} with the same shrine name, and was also revered as the general tutelary shrine of {{jalink|Kasuya-shō}}The place name Kasuya of Kasuya-shō still remains, but note that “Kami'''Kasu'''ya” and “Shimo'''Kasu'''ya” are written with different characters., including this district. Its founding date is unknown. It is listed in the '{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}' as one of 13 seats in Sagami Province and was the general tutelary shrine of 127 villages in Ōsumi District. However, {{jalink|Takamori Shrine (Isehara)|Q0|Takamori Shrine}} in Takamori, Isehara, is also considered one of the claim shrines for the Shikinaisha “Takabeya Shrine.” One reason is that this shrine is said to have been called “Takabeya Shrine” before the Meiji-era {{jalink|separation of Shinto and Buddhism order|Q1970535}}, and inscriptions reading “Takabeya Shrine” remain on timbers and on the back side of the stone monument beside its {{jalink|torii|Q234731}}[http://w1.oroti.net/~takamori/htdocs/ Sagami no Kuni Takamori Shrine] (official website), history page.. In the {{jalink|Kamakura period|Q236205}}, Kasuya Tōta Saemon no Jō Arisue ({{jalink|Kasuya Arisue|Q11604765}}), a retainer of {{jalink|Minamoto no Yoritomo|Q242800}} and local {{jalink|jitō|Q1329805}} of Kasuya-shō, built a new shrine building with Takabeya Shrine as his guardian deity. A bronze bell made in Shitoku 3 ({{jalink|1386|Q6355}}) December by Munekuni, governor of Kawachi, and donated by Taira Hidenori is designated an Important Cultural Property of Kanagawa Prefecture. In Tenbun 20 ({{jalink|1551|Q6560}}), local jitō Watanabe Iwami-no-kami restored the shrine buildings{{sfn|新編相模国風土記稿 下糟屋村 八幡宮}}. In Tenshō 19 ({{jalink|1591|Q6766}}) November, it received a red-seal grant of 10 koku of shrine land from {{jalink|Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977}}{{sfn|新編相模国風土記稿 下糟屋村 八幡宮}}. Until around the mid-Edo period, it was also called “Kasuya Hachimangū.” The main sanctuary was rebuilt in Shōhō 4 ({{jalink|1647|Q6935}}), but collapsed in the {{jalink|Great Kantō Earthquake|Q3742341}}; in Shōwa 4 ({{jalink|1929|Q18792}}) it was rebuilt reusing the columns, carvings, and front doors. The worship hall was rebuilt in Keiō 1 ({{jalink|1865|Q7708}}). The shrine preserves three old gagaku masks used in rituals from the Kamakura period onward, and a plaque reading “Hachimangū” written in early Genroku by Etsuzan Dōsō, the 7th abbot of {{jalink|Manpuku-ji|Q576318}} (Ōbaku-zan, Uji, Kyoto), a monk who came from Jinjiang County, Quanzhou Prefecture, Fujian, China. Gyōan and Gyōhaku, who donated the komainu in front of the main sanctuary, were also monks of the {{jalink|Ōbaku school|Q384728}}, suggesting a period of strong ties with Ōbaku along with the former bettō temple Jingū-ji. At present it is concurrently administered by {{jalink|Isehara Daijingū|Q11378955}}, where one may also receive {{jalink|shuin|Q7504772|seal stamps}}. === History === * {{jalink|1551|Q6560}} (Tenbun 20) - Shrine buildings rebuilt by local {{jalink|jitō|Q1329805}} Watanabe Iwami-no-kami. * {{jalink|1591|Q6766}} (Tenshō 19) - 10-koku red-seal grant donated by Tokugawa Ieyasu. * {{jalink|1865|Q7708}} (Keiō 1) - Shrine buildings restored. * {{jalink|1923|Q2169}} (Taishō 12) - Main sanctuary collapsed in the {{jalink|Great Kantō Earthquake|Q3742341}}. * {{jalink|1929|Q18792}} (Shōwa 4) - Main sanctuary rebuilt. === Rituals === [[File:Takabeya-jinja(Isehara) Kaguraden.jpg|200px|right|thumb|Kagura hall]] A ritual called “Shiokumi no Shinji” has been held in September for over 1,000 years since the {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}}. The beach sand used in the rite is taken from Terugasaki Coast, where {{jalink|Sumiyoshi Ōkami}} (Ōsumi Daimyōjin) is said to have landed. Until around the early {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} period, the shrine duty group and priest walked the 16 {{jalink|kilometer|Q828224|km}} one way to Terugasaki to collect seawater, beach sand, and hondawara seaweed (sargassum). The seawater is used as fire-quelling sacred water; the beach sand is spread on the approach for purification; the hondawara is hung on shimenawa at the worship hall and torii. === Precincts === Within the precincts are the main sanctuary, worship hall, {{jalink|kagura hall|Q94498}}, purification place, {{jalink|temizuya}}, {{jalink|bonshō|Q8192099|temple bell}}, playground equipment, and the following precinct shrines. === National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === ; Main sanctuary : Collapsed in the Great Kantō Earthquake and rebuilt in Shōwa 4. Most major timbers are reused from the pre-collapse main sanctuary rebuilt in {{jalink|Shōhō|Q1146192}} 4 (1647). Registered as a National Registered Tangible Cultural Property on February 25, 2016. ; Worship hall : Rebuilt in Keiō 1 (1865). Like the main sanctuary, registered as a National Registered Tangible Cultural Property on February 25, 2016. === Important Cultural Property of Kanagawa Prefecture === ; Bell tower : In {{jalink|Shitoku|Q1146202|Shitoku}} 3 (1386) December, Taira Hidenori donated a bronze bell. The bell is designated an Important Cultural Property of Kanagawa Prefecture. File:Takabeya-jinja(Isehara) Haiden.jpg|Worship hall File:Takabeya-jinja(Isehara) Honden.jpg|Main sanctuary File:Takabeya-jinja(Isehara) Shourou.jpg|Bell tower === Precinct shrines === * {{jalink|Yasaka Shrine (disambiguation)|Q2599651|Yasaka Shrine}} * {{jalink|Kotohira Shrine|Q11646488|Kotohira-gū}} * {{jalink|Inari Shrine}}, {{jalink|Suijin Shrine (disambiguation)||Suijin Shrine}}, {{jalink|Kōshin Shrine|Q11486003}}, {{jalink|Aizen Myōō|Q1188900}} File:Takabeya-jinja(Isehara) Yasaka-jinja.jpg|Yasaka Shrine File:Takabeya-jinja(Isehara) Konpiragu.jpg|Konpira Shrine File:Takabeya-jinja(Isehara) Aisme-myouou.jpg|Aizen Myōō File:Takabeya-jinja(Isehara) Koushinsha.jpg|Kōshin Shrine File:Takabeya-jinja(Isehara) Inarisha.jpg|Inari Shrine File:Takabeya-jinja(Isehara) Mizugami.jpg|Water deity === Images of the worship hall === ファイル:Tesshu takabeya 00.jpg|Under the eaves at the front of the worship hall is a shrine-name plaque “髙部屋神社” written by {{jalink|Yamaoka Tesshū|Q1158894}}. ファイル:Takabeya_honden.jpg|Carving on the worship-hall kōhai === Access === * About 20 minutes on foot from {{jalink|Isehara Station|Q4205219}} on the {{jalink|Odakyu Odawara Line|Q135176}}. * Bus from the station north exit on {{jalink|Kanagawa Chūō Kōtsū|Q10854960}}: ** {{jalink|I76 route|Q11588882}} bound for {{jalink|Aiko-Ishida Station|Q1107009}}, get off at “Kasuya-Kamijuku,” 3-minute walk. ** {{jalink|I74 route|Q11588882}} bound for Aiko-Ishida Station, get off at “Awakubo Iriguchi,” 4-minute walk. === Footnotes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Notes === === References === === Bibliography === * {{cite book|ja|title=Dai Nihon Chishi Taikei|volume=Vol.37 {{jalink|Shinpen Sagami no Kuni Fudokikō|Q11503942}} 2, Village Section, Ōsumi District Vol.3|chapter=Kasuya-shō Shimokasuya Village Hachimangū|id={{NDLJP|1179210/192}}|ref={{sfnref|新編相模国風土記稿 下糟屋村 八幡宮}}|publisher=Yūzankaku|date=1932-8}} === Related items === {{commonscat|Takabeya-jinja}} * {{jalink|List of Shikinaisha in Sagami Province|Q11582137}} * {{jalink|Takamori Shrine (Isehara)|Q0|Takamori Shrine}} (Takamori, Isehara) - one of the claim shrines for the Shikinaisha “Takabeya Shrine.” * {{jalink|Takabeya Village|Q11672972}} - a former village; not directly related. === External links === * [http://www.kanagawa-jinja.or.jp/search_dtl.php4?jid=819&cd=1209262&scd=&npg=0 Takabeya Shrine] (Kanagawa Prefecture Shrine Office) * [http://takabeyajinja.blog.fc2.com/ Takabeya Shrine Owl Journal] (official parishioner-run blog) * [https://sites.google.com/view/koujinkai-kagumi-isehara/ Isehara Kōshinkai] (official site of Takabeya Shrine Mikoshi Preservation Association) {{Shinto-stub}} {{Pref-stub|pref=神奈川県}} (P9721) == * 高部屋神社.jpg {{commons category|Category:Takabeya-jinja}} [[JA:高部屋神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11672973}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Takabeya Shrine |画像 = [[File:高部屋神社.jpg|250px]]
Torii and worship hall |所在地 = {{jalink|Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513}}{{jalink|Isehara|Q734520}} Shimokasuya 2202 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = Kamuyamatoiwarehiko-no-Mikoto, Hondawake-no-Mikoto, Mitsutsuo-no-Mikoto, Ōsasagi-no-Mikoto, Okinagatarashihime-no-Mikoto, Iwanohime-no-Mikoto |社格 = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (minor), {{jalink|Village Shrine|Q134917284}} (former {{jalink|modern shrine rank system|Q712534|shrine rank}}) |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = |別名 = Hachiman Shrine (Kasuya Hachiman) |例祭 = {{jalink|September 15|Q2846}} |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Kanagawa|label=Takabeya Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Takabeya Shrine''' (たかべやじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in Shimokasuya, {{jalink|Isehara|Q734520}}, {{jalink|Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513}}. It is considered one of the 13 Engishiki shrines (minor shrine) in {{jalink|Sagami Province|Q1053732}} listed in the '{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}}' of Enchō 5 ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}){{sfn|新編相模国風土記稿 下糟屋村 八幡宮}}, though as noted below there are also competing claim shrines ({{jalink|ronsha|Q135022904}}). Its former shrine rank was Village Shrine. === Overview === It was formerly the village shrine of {{jalink|Takabeya Village|Q11672972}} in {{jalink|Ōsumi District|Q11432367}} with the same shrine name, and was also revered as the general tutelary shrine of {{jalink|Kasuya-shō}}The place name Kasuya of Kasuya-shō still remains, but note that “Kami'''Kasu'''ya” and “Shimo'''Kasu'''ya” are written with different characters., including this district. Its founding date is unknown. It is listed in the '{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}' as one of 13 seats in Sagami Province and was the general tutelary shrine of 127 villages in Ōsumi District. However, {{jalink|Takamori Shrine (Isehara)|Q0|Takamori Shrine}} in Takamori, Isehara, is also considered one of the claim shrines for the Shikinaisha “Takabeya Shrine.” One reason is that this shrine is said to have been called “Takabeya Shrine” before the Meiji-era {{jalink|separation of Shinto and Buddhism order|Q1970535}}, and inscriptions reading “Takabeya Shrine” remain on timbers and on the back side of the stone monument beside its {{jalink|torii|Q234731}}[http://w1.oroti.net/~takamori/htdocs/ Sagami no Kuni Takamori Shrine] (official website), history page.. In the {{jalink|Kamakura period|Q236205}}, Kasuya Tōta Saemon no Jō Arisue ({{jalink|Kasuya Arisue|Q11604765}}), a retainer of {{jalink|Minamoto no Yoritomo|Q242800}} and local {{jalink|jitō|Q1329805}} of Kasuya-shō, built a new shrine building with Takabeya Shrine as his guardian deity. A bronze bell made in Shitoku 3 ({{jalink|1386|Q6355}}) December by Munekuni, governor of Kawachi, and donated by Taira Hidenori is designated an Important Cultural Property of Kanagawa Prefecture. In Tenbun 20 ({{jalink|1551|Q6560}}), local jitō Watanabe Iwami-no-kami restored the shrine buildings{{sfn|新編相模国風土記稿 下糟屋村 八幡宮}}. In Tenshō 19 ({{jalink|1591|Q6766}}) November, it received a red-seal grant of 10 koku of shrine land from {{jalink|Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977}}{{sfn|新編相模国風土記稿 下糟屋村 八幡宮}}. Until around the mid-Edo period, it was also called “Kasuya Hachimangū.” The main sanctuary was rebuilt in Shōhō 4 ({{jalink|1647|Q6935}}), but collapsed in the {{jalink|Great Kantō Earthquake|Q3742341}}; in Shōwa 4 ({{jalink|1929|Q18792}}) it was rebuilt reusing the columns, carvings, and front doors. The worship hall was rebuilt in Keiō 1 ({{jalink|1865|Q7708}}). The shrine preserves three old gagaku masks used in rituals from the Kamakura period onward, and a plaque reading “Hachimangū” written in early Genroku by Etsuzan Dōsō, the 7th abbot of {{jalink|Manpuku-ji|Q576318}} (Ōbaku-zan, Uji, Kyoto), a monk who came from Jinjiang County, Quanzhou Prefecture, Fujian, China. Gyōan and Gyōhaku, who donated the komainu in front of the main sanctuary, were also monks of the {{jalink|Ōbaku school|Q384728}}, suggesting a period of strong ties with Ōbaku along with the former bettō temple Jingū-ji. At present it is concurrently administered by {{jalink|Isehara Daijingū|Q11378955}}, where one may also receive {{jalink|shuin|Q7504772|seal stamps}}. === History === * {{jalink|1551|Q6560}} (Tenbun 20) - Shrine buildings rebuilt by local {{jalink|jitō|Q1329805}} Watanabe Iwami-no-kami. * {{jalink|1591|Q6766}} (Tenshō 19) - 10-koku red-seal grant donated by Tokugawa Ieyasu. * {{jalink|1865|Q7708}} (Keiō 1) - Shrine buildings restored. * {{jalink|1923|Q2169}} (Taishō 12) - Main sanctuary collapsed in the {{jalink|Great Kantō Earthquake|Q3742341}}. * {{jalink|1929|Q18792}} (Shōwa 4) - Main sanctuary rebuilt. === Rituals === [[File:Takabeya-jinja(Isehara) Kaguraden.jpg|200px|right|thumb|Kagura hall]] A ritual called “Shiokumi no Shinji” has been held in September for over 1,000 years since the {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}}. The beach sand used in the rite is taken from Terugasaki Coast, where {{jalink|Sumiyoshi Ōkami}} (Ōsumi Daimyōjin) is said to have landed. Until around the early {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} period, the shrine duty group and priest walked the 16 {{jalink|kilometer|Q828224|km}} one way to Terugasaki to collect seawater, beach sand, and hondawara seaweed (sargassum). The seawater is used as fire-quelling sacred water; the beach sand is spread on the approach for purification; the hondawara is hung on shimenawa at the worship hall and torii. === Precincts === Within the precincts are the main sanctuary, worship hall, {{jalink|kagura hall|Q94498}}, purification place, {{jalink|temizuya}}, {{jalink|bonshō|Q8192099|temple bell}}, playground equipment, and the following precinct shrines. === National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === ; Main sanctuary : Collapsed in the Great Kantō Earthquake and rebuilt in Shōwa 4. Most major timbers are reused from the pre-collapse main sanctuary rebuilt in {{jalink|Shōhō|Q1146192}} 4 (1647). Registered as a National Registered Tangible Cultural Property on February 25, 2016. ; Worship hall : Rebuilt in Keiō 1 (1865). Like the main sanctuary, registered as a National Registered Tangible Cultural Property on February 25, 2016. === Important Cultural Property of Kanagawa Prefecture === ; Bell tower : In {{jalink|Shitoku|Q1146202|Shitoku}} 3 (1386) December, Taira Hidenori donated a bronze bell. The bell is designated an Important Cultural Property of Kanagawa Prefecture. (P9721) == * 高部屋神社.jpg {{commons category|Category:Takabeya-jinja}} [[JA:高部屋神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11672973}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Takabeya Shrine |画像 = [[File:高部屋神社.jpg|250px]]
Torii and worship hall |所在地 = {{jalink|Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513}}{{jalink|Isehara|Q734520}} Shimokasuya 2202 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = Kamuyamatoiwarehiko-no-Mikoto, Hondawake-no-Mikoto, Mitsutsuo-no-Mikoto, Ōsasagi-no-Mikoto, Okinagatarashihime-no-Mikoto, Iwanohime-no-Mikoto |社格 = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (minor), {{jalink|Village Shrine|Q134917284}} (former {{jalink|modern shrine rank system|Q712534|shrine rank}}) |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = |別名 = Hachiman Shrine (Kasuya Hachiman) |例祭 = {{jalink|September 15|Q2846}} |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Kanagawa|label=Takabeya Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Takabeya Shrine''' (たかべやじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in Shimokasuya, {{jalink|Isehara|Q734520}}, {{jalink|Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513}}. It is considered one of the 13 Engishiki shrines (minor shrine) in {{jalink|Sagami Province|Q1053732}} listed in the '{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}}' of Enchō 5 ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}){{sfn|新編相模国風土記稿 下糟屋村 八幡宮}}, though as noted below there are also competing claim shrines ({{jalink|ronsha|Q135022904}}). Its former shrine rank was Village Shrine. === Overview === It was formerly the village shrine of {{jalink|Takabeya Village|Q11672972}} in {{jalink|Ōsumi District|Q11432367}} with the same shrine name, and was also revered as the general tutelary shrine of {{jalink|Kasuya-shō}}The place name Kasuya of Kasuya-shō still remains, but note that “Kami'''Kasu'''ya” and “Shimo'''Kasu'''ya” are written with different characters., including this district. Its founding date is unknown. It is listed in the '{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}' as one of 13 seats in Sagami Province and was the general tutelary shrine of 127 villages in Ōsumi District. However, {{jalink|Takamori Shrine (Isehara)|Q0|Takamori Shrine}} in Takamori, Isehara, is also considered one of the claim shrines for the Shikinaisha “Takabeya Shrine.” One reason is that this shrine is said to have been called “Takabeya Shrine” before the Meiji-era {{jalink|separation of Shinto and Buddhism order|Q1970535}}, and inscriptions reading “Takabeya Shrine” remain on timbers and on the back side of the stone monument beside its {{jalink|torii|Q234731}}[http://w1.oroti.net/~takamori/htdocs/ Sagami no Kuni Takamori Shrine] (official website), history page.. In the {{jalink|Kamakura period|Q236205}}, Kasuya Tōta Saemon no Jō Arisue ({{jalink|Kasuya Arisue|Q11604765}}), a retainer of {{jalink|Minamoto no Yoritomo|Q242800}} and local {{jalink|jitō|Q1329805}} of Kasuya-shō, built a new shrine building with Takabeya Shrine as his guardian deity. A bronze bell made in Shitoku 3 ({{jalink|1386|Q6355}}) December by Munekuni, governor of Kawachi, and donated by Taira Hidenori is designated an Important Cultural Property of Kanagawa Prefecture. In Tenbun 20 ({{jalink|1551|Q6560}}), local jitō Watanabe Iwami-no-kami restored the shrine buildings{{sfn|新編相模国風土記稿 下糟屋村 八幡宮}}. In Tenshō 19 ({{jalink|1591|Q6766}}) November, it received a red-seal grant of 10 koku of shrine land from {{jalink|Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977}}{{sfn|新編相模国風土記稿 下糟屋村 八幡宮}}. Until around the mid-Edo period, it was also called “Kasuya Hachimangū.” The main sanctuary was rebuilt in Shōhō 4 ({{jalink|1647|Q6935}}), but collapsed in the {{jalink|Great Kantō Earthquake|Q3742341}}; in Shōwa 4 ({{jalink|1929|Q18792}}) it was rebuilt reusing the columns, carvings, and front doors. The worship hall was rebuilt in Keiō 1 ({{jalink|1865|Q7708}}). The shrine preserves three old gagaku masks used in rituals from the Kamakura period onward, and a plaque reading “Hachimangū” written in early Genroku by Etsuzan Dōsō, the 7th abbot of {{jalink|Manpuku-ji|Q576318}} (Ōbaku-zan, Uji, Kyoto), a monk who came from Jinjiang County, Quanzhou Prefecture, Fujian, China. Gyōan and Gyōhaku, who donated the komainu in front of the main sanctuary, were also monks of the {{jalink|Ōbaku school|Q384728}}, suggesting a period of strong ties with Ōbaku along with the former bettō temple Jingū-ji. At present it is concurrently administered by {{jalink|Isehara Daijingū|Q11378955}}, where one may also receive {{jalink|shuin|Q7504772|seal stamps}}. === History === * {{jalink|1551|Q6560}} (Tenbun 20) - Shrine buildings rebuilt by local {{jalink|jitō|Q1329805}} Watanabe Iwami-no-kami. * {{jalink|1591|Q6766}} (Tenshō 19) - 10-koku red-seal grant donated by Tokugawa Ieyasu. * {{jalink|1865|Q7708}} (Keiō 1) - Shrine buildings restored. * {{jalink|1923|Q2169}} (Taishō 12) - Main sanctuary collapsed in the {{jalink|Great Kantō Earthquake|Q3742341}}. * {{jalink|1929|Q18792}} (Shōwa 4) - Main sanctuary rebuilt. === Rituals === [[File:Takabeya-jinja(Isehara) Kaguraden.jpg|200px|right|thumb|Kagura hall]] A ritual called “Shiokumi no Shinji” has been held in September for over 1,000 years since the {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}}. The beach sand used in the rite is taken from Terugasaki Coast, where {{jalink|Sumiyoshi Ōkami}} (Ōsumi Daimyōjin) is said to have landed. Until around the early {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} period, the shrine duty group and priest walked the 16 {{jalink|kilometer|Q828224|km}} one way to Terugasaki to collect seawater, beach sand, and hondawara seaweed (sargassum). The seawater is used as fire-quelling sacred water; the beach sand is spread on the approach for purification; the hondawara is hung on shimenawa at the worship hall and torii. === Precincts === Within the precincts are the main sanctuary, worship hall, {{jalink|kagura hall|Q94498}}, purification place, {{jalink|temizuya}}, {{jalink|bonshō|Q8192099|temple bell}}, playground equipment, and the following precinct shrines. === National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === ; Main sanctuary : Collapsed in the Great Kantō Earthquake and rebuilt in Shōwa 4. Most major timbers are reused from the pre-collapse main sanctuary rebuilt in {{jalink|Shōhō|Q1146192}} 4 (1647). Registered as a National Registered Tangible Cultural Property on February 25, 2016. ; Worship hall : Rebuilt in Keiō 1 (1865). Like the main sanctuary, registered as a National Registered Tangible Cultural Property on February 25, 2016. === Important Cultural Property of Kanagawa Prefecture === ; Bell tower : In {{jalink|Shitoku|Q1146202|Shitoku}} 3 (1386) December, Taira Hidenori donated a bronze bell. The bell is designated an Important Cultural Property of Kanagawa Prefecture. (P9721) == * 高部屋神社.jpg {{commons category|Category:Takabeya-jinja}} [[JA:高部屋神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11672973}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Takabeya Shrine |画像 = [[File:高部屋神社.jpg|250px]]
Torii and worship hall |所在地 = {{jalink|Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513}}{{jalink|Isehara|Q734520}} Shimokasuya 2202 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = Kamuyamatoiwarehiko-no-Mikoto, Hondawake-no-Mikoto, Mitsutsuo-no-Mikoto, Ōsasagi-no-Mikoto, Okinagatarashihime-no-Mikoto, Iwanohime-no-Mikoto |社格 = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (minor), {{jalink|Village Shrine|Q134917284}} (former {{jalink|modern shrine rank system|Q712534|shrine rank}}) |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = |別名 = Hachiman Shrine (Kasuya Hachiman) |例祭 = {{jalink|September 15|Q2846}} |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Kanagawa|label=Takabeya Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Takabeya Shrine''' (たかべやじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in Shimokasuya, {{jalink|Isehara|Q734520}}, {{jalink|Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513}}. It is considered one of the 13 Engishiki shrines (minor shrine) in {{jalink|Sagami Province|Q1053732}} listed in the '{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}}' of Enchō 5 ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}){{sfn|新編相模国風土記稿 下糟屋村 八幡宮}}, though as noted below there are also competing claim shrines ({{jalink|ronsha|Q135022904}}). Its former shrine rank was Village Shrine. === Overview === It was formerly the village shrine of {{jalink|Takabeya Village|Q11672972}} in {{jalink|Ōsumi District|Q11432367}} with the same shrine name, and was also revered as the general tutelary shrine of {{jalink|Kasuya-shō}}The place name Kasuya of Kasuya-shō still remains, but note that “Kami'''Kasu'''ya” and “Shimo'''Kasu'''ya” are written with different characters., including this district. Its founding date is unknown. It is listed in the '{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}' as one of 13 seats in Sagami Province and was the general tutelary shrine of 127 villages in Ōsumi District. However, {{jalink|Takamori Shrine (Isehara)|Q0|Takamori Shrine}} in Takamori, Isehara, is also considered one of the claim shrines for the Shikinaisha “Takabeya Shrine.” One reason is that this shrine is said to have been called “Takabeya Shrine” before the Meiji-era {{jalink|separation of Shinto and Buddhism order|Q1970535}}, and inscriptions reading “Takabeya Shrine” remain on timbers and on the back side of the stone monument beside its {{jalink|torii|Q234731}}[http://w1.oroti.net/~takamori/htdocs/ Sagami no Kuni Takamori Shrine] (official website), history page.. In the {{jalink|Kamakura period|Q236205}}, Kasuya Tōta Saemon no Jō Arisue ({{jalink|Kasuya Arisue|Q11604765}}), a retainer of {{jalink|Minamoto no Yoritomo|Q242800}} and local {{jalink|jitō|Q1329805}} of Kasuya-shō, built a new shrine building with Takabeya Shrine as his guardian deity. A bronze bell made in Shitoku 3 ({{jalink|1386|Q6355}}) December by Munekuni, governor of Kawachi, and donated by Taira Hidenori is designated an Important Cultural Property of Kanagawa Prefecture. In Tenbun 20 ({{jalink|1551|Q6560}}), local jitō Watanabe Iwami-no-kami restored the shrine buildings{{sfn|新編相模国風土記稿 下糟屋村 八幡宮}}. In Tenshō 19 ({{jalink|1591|Q6766}}) November, it received a red-seal grant of 10 koku of shrine land from {{jalink|Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977}}{{sfn|新編相模国風土記稿 下糟屋村 八幡宮}}. Until around the mid-Edo period, it was also called “Kasuya Hachimangū.” The main sanctuary was rebuilt in Shōhō 4 ({{jalink|1647|Q6935}}), but collapsed in the {{jalink|Great Kantō Earthquake|Q3742341}}; in Shōwa 4 ({{jalink|1929|Q18792}}) it was rebuilt reusing the columns, carvings, and front doors. The worship hall was rebuilt in Keiō 1 ({{jalink|1865|Q7708}}). The shrine preserves three old gagaku masks used in rituals from the Kamakura period onward, and a plaque reading “Hachimangū” written in early Genroku by Etsuzan Dōsō, the 7th abbot of {{jalink|Manpuku-ji|Q576318}} (Ōbaku-zan, Uji, Kyoto), a monk who came from Jinjiang County, Quanzhou Prefecture, Fujian, China. Gyōan and Gyōhaku, who donated the komainu in front of the main sanctuary, were also monks of the {{jalink|Ōbaku school|Q384728}}, suggesting a period of strong ties with Ōbaku along with the former bettō temple Jingū-ji. At present it is concurrently administered by {{jalink|Isehara Daijingū|Q11378955}}, where one may also receive {{jalink|shuin|Q7504772|seal stamps}}. === History === * {{jalink|1551|Q6560}} (Tenbun 20) - Shrine buildings rebuilt by local {{jalink|jitō|Q1329805}} Watanabe Iwami-no-kami. * {{jalink|1591|Q6766}} (Tenshō 19) - 10-koku red-seal grant donated by Tokugawa Ieyasu. * {{jalink|1865|Q7708}} (Keiō 1) - Shrine buildings restored. * {{jalink|1923|Q2169}} (Taishō 12) - Main sanctuary collapsed in the {{jalink|Great Kantō Earthquake|Q3742341}}. * {{jalink|1929|Q18792}} (Shōwa 4) - Main sanctuary rebuilt. === Rituals === [[File:Takabeya-jinja(Isehara) Kaguraden.jpg|200px|right|thumb|Kagura hall]] A ritual called “Shiokumi no Shinji” has been held in September for over 1,000 years since the {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}}. The beach sand used in the rite is taken from Terugasaki Coast, where {{jalink|Sumiyoshi Ōkami}} (Ōsumi Daimyōjin) is said to have landed. Until around the early {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} period, the shrine duty group and priest walked the 16 {{jalink|kilometer|Q828224|km}} one way to Terugasaki to collect seawater, beach sand, and hondawara seaweed (sargassum). The seawater is used as fire-quelling sacred water; the beach sand is spread on the approach for purification; the hondawara is hung on shimenawa at the worship hall and torii. === Precincts === Within the precincts are the main sanctuary, worship hall, {{jalink|kagura hall|Q94498}}, purification place, {{jalink|temizuya}}, {{jalink|bonshō|Q8192099|temple bell}}, playground equipment, and the following precinct shrines. === National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === ; Main sanctuary : Collapsed in the Great Kantō Earthquake and rebuilt in Shōwa 4. Most major timbers are reused from the pre-collapse main sanctuary rebuilt in {{jalink|Shōhō|Q1146192}} 4 (1647). Registered as a National Registered Tangible Cultural Property on February 25, 2016. ; Worship hall : Rebuilt in Keiō 1 (1865). Like the main sanctuary, registered as a National Registered Tangible Cultural Property on February 25, 2016. === Important Cultural Property of Kanagawa Prefecture === ; Bell tower : In {{jalink|Shitoku|Q1146202|Shitoku}} 3 (1386) December, Taira Hidenori donated a bronze bell. The bell is designated an Important Cultural Property of Kanagawa Prefecture. “Kasuya-Kamijuku,” 3-minute walk. ** {{jalink|I74 route|Q11588882}} bound for Aiko-Ishida Station, get off at “Awakubo Iriguchi,” 4-minute walk. === Footnotes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Notes === === (P9721) == * 高部屋神社.jpg {{commons category|Category:Takabeya-jinja}} [[JA:高部屋神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11672973}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Takabeya Shrine |画像 = [[File:高部屋神社.jpg|250px]]
Torii and worship hall |所在地 = {{jalink|Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513}}{{jalink|Isehara|Q734520}} Shimokasuya 2202 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = Kamuyamatoiwarehiko-no-Mikoto, Hondawake-no-Mikoto, Mitsutsuo-no-Mikoto, Ōsasagi-no-Mikoto, Okinagatarashihime-no-Mikoto, Iwanohime-no-Mikoto |社格 = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (minor), {{jalink|Village Shrine|Q134917284}} (former {{jalink|modern shrine rank system|Q712534|shrine rank}}) |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = |別名 = Hachiman Shrine (Kasuya Hachiman) |例祭 = {{jalink|September 15|Q2846}} |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Kanagawa|label=Takabeya Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Takabeya Shrine''' (たかべやじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in Shimokasuya, {{jalink|Isehara|Q734520}}, {{jalink|Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513}}. It is considered one of the 13 Engishiki shrines (minor shrine) in {{jalink|Sagami Province|Q1053732}} listed in the '{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}}' of Enchō 5 ({{jalink|927|Q30470}}){{sfn|新編相模国風土記稿 下糟屋村 八幡宮}}, though as noted below there are also competing claim shrines ({{jalink|ronsha|Q135022904}}). Its former shrine rank was Village Shrine. === Overview === It was formerly the village shrine of {{jalink|Takabeya Village|Q11672972}} in {{jalink|Ōsumi District|Q11432367}} with the same shrine name, and was also revered as the general tutelary shrine of {{jalink|Kasuya-shō}}The place name Kasuya of Kasuya-shō still remains, but note that “Kami'''Kasu'''ya” and “Shimo'''Kasu'''ya” are written with different characters., including this district. Its founding date is unknown. It is listed in the '{{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}' as one of 13 seats in Sagami Province and was the general tutelary shrine of 127 villages in Ōsumi District. However, {{jalink|Takamori Shrine (Isehara)|Q0|Takamori Shrine}} in Takamori, Isehara, is also considered one of the claim shrines for the Shikinaisha “Takabeya Shrine.” One reason is that this shrine is said to have been called “Takabeya Shrine” before the Meiji-era {{jalink|separation of Shinto and Buddhism order|Q1970535}}, and inscriptions reading “Takabeya Shrine” remain on timbers and on the back side of the stone monument beside its {{jalink|torii|Q234731}}[http://w1.oroti.net/~takamori/htdocs/ Sagami no Kuni Takamori Shrine] (official website), history page.. In the {{jalink|Kamakura period|Q236205}}, Kasuya Tōta Saemon no Jō Arisue ({{jalink|Kasuya Arisue|Q11604765}}), a retainer of {{jalink|Minamoto no Yoritomo|Q242800}} and local {{jalink|jitō|Q1329805}} of Kasuya-shō, built a new shrine building with Takabeya Shrine as his guardian deity. A bronze bell made in Shitoku 3 ({{jalink|1386|Q6355}}) December by Munekuni, governor of Kawachi, and donated by Taira Hidenori is designated an Important Cultural Property of Kanagawa Prefecture. In Tenbun 20 ({{jalink|1551|Q6560}}), local jitō Watanabe Iwami-no-kami restored the shrine buildings{{sfn|新編相模国風土記稿 下糟屋村 八幡宮}}. In Tenshō 19 ({{jalink|1591|Q6766}}) November, it received a red-seal grant of 10 koku of shrine land from {{jalink|Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977}}{{sfn|新編相模国風土記稿 下糟屋村 八幡宮}}. Until around the mid-Edo period, it was also called “Kasuya Hachimangū.” The main sanctuary was rebuilt in Shōhō 4 ({{jalink|1647|Q6935}}), but collapsed in the {{jalink|Great Kantō Earthquake|Q3742341}}; in Shōwa 4 ({{jalink|1929|Q18792}}) it was rebuilt reusing the columns, carvings, and front doors. The worship hall was rebuilt in Keiō 1 ({{jalink|1865|Q7708}}). The shrine preserves three old gagaku masks used in rituals from the Kamakura period onward, and a plaque reading “Hachimangū” written in early Genroku by Etsuzan Dōsō, the 7th abbot of {{jalink|Manpuku-ji|Q576318}} (Ōbaku-zan, Uji, Kyoto), a monk who came from Jinjiang County, Quanzhou Prefecture, Fujian, China. Gyōan and Gyōhaku, who donated the komainu in front of the main sanctuary, were also monks of the {{jalink|Ōbaku school|Q384728}}, suggesting a period of strong ties with Ōbaku along with the former bettō temple Jingū-ji. At present it is concurrently administered by {{jalink|Isehara Daijingū|Q11378955}}, where one may also receive {{jalink|shuin|Q7504772|seal stamps}}. === History === * {{jalink|1551|Q6560}} (Tenbun 20) - Shrine buildings rebuilt by local {{jalink|jitō|Q1329805}} Watanabe Iwami-no-kami. * {{jalink|1591|Q6766}} (Tenshō 19) - 10-koku red-seal grant donated by Tokugawa Ieyasu. * {{jalink|1865|Q7708}} (Keiō 1) - Shrine buildings restored. * {{jalink|1923|Q2169}} (Taishō 12) - Main sanctuary collapsed in the {{jalink|Great Kantō Earthquake|Q3742341}}. * {{jalink|1929|Q18792}} (Shōwa 4) - Main sanctuary rebuilt. === Rituals === [[File:Takabeya-jinja(Isehara) Kaguraden.jpg|200px|right|thumb|Kagura hall]] A ritual called “Shiokumi no Shinji” has been held in September for over 1,000 years since the {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}}. The beach sand used in the rite is taken from Terugasaki Coast, where {{jalink|Sumiyoshi Ōkami}} (Ōsumi Daimyōjin) is said to have landed. Until around the early {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} period, the shrine duty group and priest walked the 16 {{jalink|kilometer|Q828224|km}} one way to Terugasaki to collect seawater, beach sand, and hondawara seaweed (sargassum). The seawater is used as fire-quelling sacred water; the beach sand is spread on the approach for purification; the hondawara is hung on shimenawa at the worship hall and torii. === Precincts === Within the precincts are the main sanctuary, worship hall, {{jalink|kagura hall|Q94498}}, purification place, {{jalink|temizuya}}, {{jalink|bonshō|Q8192099|temple bell}}, playground equipment, and the following precinct shrines. === National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === ; Main sanctuary : Collapsed in the Great Kantō Earthquake and rebuilt in Shōwa 4. Most major timbers are reused from the pre-collapse main sanctuary rebuilt in {{jalink|Shōhō|Q1146192}} 4 (1647). Registered as a National Registered Tangible Cultural Property on February 25, 2016. ; Worship hall : Rebuilt in Keiō 1 (1865). Like the main sanctuary, registered as a National Registered Tangible Cultural Property on February 25, 2016. === Important Cultural Property of Kanagawa Prefecture === ; Bell tower : In {{jalink|Shitoku|Q1146202|Shitoku}} 3 (1386) December, Taira Hidenori donated a bronze bell. The bell is designated an Important Cultural Property of Kanagawa Prefecture. Tesshū|Q1158894}}. ファイル:Takabeya_honden.jpg|Carving on the worship-hall kōhai === Access === * About 20 minutes on foot from {{jalink|Isehara Station|Q4205219}} on the {{jalink|Odakyu Odawara Line|Q135176}}. * Bus from the station north exit on {{jalink|Kanagawa Chūō Kōtsū|Q10854960}}: ** {{jalink|I76 route|Q11588882}} bound for {{jalink|Aiko-Ishida Station|Q1107009}}, get off at “Kasuya-Kamijuku,” 3-minute walk. ** {{jalink|I74 route|Q11588882}} bound for Aiko-Ishida Station, get off at “Awakubo Iriguchi,” 4-minute walk. === Footnotes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Notes === ===
