Images of the worship hall === ファイル:Tesshu takabeya 00.jpg|Under the eaves at the front of the worship hall is a shrine-name plaque “髙部屋神社” written by {{ill|lt=Yamaoka Tesshū|Q1158894|WD=Q1158894|1=Yamaoka Tesshū}}. ファイル:Takabeya_honden.jpg|Carving on the worship-hall kōhai === Access === * About 20 minutes on foot from {{ill|lt=Isehara Station|Q4205219|WD=Q4205219|1=Isehara Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Odakyu Odawara Line|Q135176|WD=Q135176|1=Odakyū Odawara Line}}. * Bus from the station north exit on {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Chūō Kōtsū|Q10854960|WD=Q10854960|1=Kanagawa Chuo Kotsu}}: ** {{ill|lt=I76 route|Q11588882|WD=Q11588882|1=I76 route}} bound for {{ill|lt=Aiko-Ishida Station|Q1107009|WD=Q1107009|1=Aikō-Ishida Station}}, get off at “Kasuya-Kamijuku,” 3-minute walk. ** {{ill|lt=I74 route|Q11588882|WD=Q11588882|1=I74 route}} bound for Aiko-Ishida Station, get off at “Awakubo Iriguchi,” 4-minute walk. === Footnotes === content == {{神社 |名称 = Takabeya Shrine |画像 = [[File:高部屋神社.jpg|250px]]
Torii and worship hall |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513|WD=Q127513|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Isehara|Q734520|WD=Q734520|1=Isehara, Kanagawa}} Shimokasuya 2202 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = Kamuyamatoiwarehiko-no-Mikoto, Hondawake-no-Mikoto, Mitsutsuo-no-Mikoto, Ōsasagi-no-Mikoto, Okinagatarashihime-no-Mikoto, Iwanohime-no-Mikoto |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (minor), {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} (former {{ill|lt=modern shrine rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}) |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = |別名 = Hachiman Shrine (Kasuya Hachiman) |例祭 = {{ill|lt=September 15|Q2846|WD=Q2846|1=September 15}} |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Kanagawa|label=Takabeya Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Takabeya Shrine''' (たかべやじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Shimokasuya, {{ill|lt=Isehara|Q734520|WD=Q734520|1=Isehara, Kanagawa}}, {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513|WD=Q127513|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}}. It is considered one of the 13 Engishiki shrines (minor shrine) in {{ill|lt=Sagami Province|Q1053732|WD=Q1053732|1=Sagami Province}} listed in the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}' of Enchō 5 ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}}){{sfn|新編相模国風土記稿 下糟屋村 八幡宮}}, though as noted below there are also competing claim shrines ({{ill|lt=ronsha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}}). Its former shrine rank was Village Shrine. === Overview === It was formerly the village shrine of {{ill|lt=Takabeya Village|Q11672972|WD=Q11672972|1=Takabeya}} in {{ill|lt=Ōsumi District|Q11432367|WD=Q11432367|1=Ōsumi district}} with the same shrine name, and was also revered as the general tutelary shrine of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kasuya-shō|1=UNKNOWN}}The place name Kasuya of Kasuya-shō still remains, but note that “Kami'''Kasu'''ya” and “Shimo'''Kasu'''ya” are written with different characters., including this district. Its founding date is unknown. It is listed in the '{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=UNKNOWN}}' as one of 13 seats in Sagami Province and was the general tutelary shrine of 127 villages in Ōsumi District. However, {{ill|lt=Takamori Shrine (Isehara)|Q0|WD=Q0|lt=Takamori Shrine|1=Takamori Shrine}} in Takamori, Isehara, is also considered one of the claim shrines for the Shikinaisha “Takabeya Shrine.” One reason is that this shrine is said to have been called “Takabeya Shrine” before the Meiji-era {{ill|lt=separation of Shinto and Buddhism order|Q1970535|WD=Q1970535|1=Shinbutsu bunri}}, and inscriptions reading “Takabeya Shrine” remain on timbers and on the back side of the stone monument beside its {{ill|lt=torii|Q234731|WD=Q234731|1=Torii}}[http://w1.oroti.net/~takamori/htdocs/ Sagami no Kuni Takamori Shrine] (official website), history page.. In the {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205|1=Kamakura period}}, Kasuya Tōta Saemon no Jō Arisue ({{ill|lt=Kasuya Arisue|Q11604765|WD=Q11604765|1=Kasuya Arisue}}), a retainer of {{ill|lt=Minamoto no Yoritomo|Q242800|WD=Q242800|1=Minamoto no Yoritomo}} and local {{ill|lt=jitō|Q1329805|WD=Q1329805|1=Jitō}} of Kasuya-shō, built a new shrine building with Takabeya Shrine as his guardian deity. A bronze bell made in Shitoku 3 ({{ill|lt=1386|Q6355|WD=Q6355|1=1386}}) December by Munekuni, governor of Kawachi, and donated by Taira Hidenori is designated an Important Cultural Property of Kanagawa Prefecture. In Tenbun 20 ({{ill|lt=1551|Q6560|WD=Q6560|1=1551}}), local jitō Watanabe Iwami-no-kami restored the shrine buildings{{sfn|新編相模国風土記稿 下糟屋村 八幡宮}}. In Tenshō 19 ({{ill|lt=1591|Q6766|WD=Q6766|1=1591}}) November, it received a red-seal grant of 10 koku of shrine land from {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977|WD=Q171977|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}{{sfn|新編相模国風土記稿 下糟屋村 八幡宮}}. Until around the mid-Edo period, it was also called “Kasuya Hachimangū.” The main sanctuary was rebuilt in Shōhō 4 ({{ill|lt=1647|Q6935|WD=Q6935|1=1647}}), but collapsed in the {{ill|lt=Great Kantō Earthquake|Q3742341|WD=Q3742341|1=aftermath of 1923 Great Kantō earthquake}}; in Shōwa 4 ({{ill|lt=1929|Q18792|WD=Q18792|1=1929}}) it was rebuilt reusing the columns, carvings, and front doors. The worship hall was rebuilt in Keiō 1 ({{ill|lt=1865|Q7708|WD=Q7708|1=1865}}). The shrine preserves three old gagaku masks used in rituals from the Kamakura period onward, and a plaque reading “Hachimangū” written in early Genroku by Etsuzan Dōsō, the 7th abbot of {{ill|lt=Manpuku-ji|Q576318|WD=Q576318|1=Manpuku-ji}} (Ōbaku-zan, Uji, Kyoto), a monk who came from Jinjiang County, Quanzhou Prefecture, Fujian, China. Gyōan and Gyōhaku, who donated the komainu in front of the main sanctuary, were also monks of the {{ill|lt=Ōbaku school|Q384728|WD=Q384728|1=Ōbaku}}, suggesting a period of strong ties with Ōbaku along with the former bettō temple Jingū-ji. At present it is concurrently administered by {{ill|lt=Isehara Daijingū|Q11378955|WD=Q11378955|1=Isehara Daijingu}}, where one may also receive {{ill|lt=shuin|Q7504772|WD=Q7504772|lt=seal stamps|1=Shuin}}. === History === * {{ill|lt=1551|Q6560|WD=Q6560|1=1551}} (Tenbun 20) - Shrine buildings rebuilt by local {{ill|lt=jitō|Q1329805|WD=Q1329805|1=Jitō}} Watanabe Iwami-no-kami. * {{ill|lt=1591|Q6766|WD=Q6766|1=1591}} (Tenshō 19) - 10-koku red-seal grant donated by Tokugawa Ieyasu. * {{ill|lt=1865|Q7708|WD=Q7708|1=1865}} (Keiō 1) - Shrine buildings restored. * {{ill|lt=1923|Q2169|WD=Q2169|1=1923}} (Taishō 12) - Main sanctuary collapsed in the {{ill|lt=Great Kantō Earthquake|Q3742341|WD=Q3742341|1=aftermath of 1923 Great Kantō earthquake}}. * {{ill|lt=1929|Q18792|WD=Q18792|1=1929}} (Shōwa 4) - Main sanctuary rebuilt. === Rituals === [[File:Takabeya-jinja(Isehara) Kaguraden.jpg|200px|right|thumb|Kagura hall]] A ritual called “Shiokumi no Shinji” has been held in September for over 1,000 years since the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292|1=Heian period}}. The beach sand used in the rite is taken from Terugasaki Coast, where {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sumiyoshi Ōkami|WD=Q1114013|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin}} (Ōsumi Daimyōjin) is said to have landed. Until around the early {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} period, the shrine duty group and priest walked the 16 {{ill|lt=kilometer|Q828224|WD=Q828224|lt=km|1=Kilometre}} one way to Terugasaki to collect seawater, beach sand, and hondawara seaweed (sargassum). The seawater is used as fire-quelling sacred water; the beach sand is spread on the approach for purification; the hondawara is hung on shimenawa at the worship hall and torii. === Precincts === Within the precincts are the main sanctuary, worship hall, {{ill|lt=kagura hall|Q94498|WD=Q94498|1=Kagura-den}}, purification place, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=temizuya|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=bonshō|Q8192099|WD=Q8192099|lt=temple bell|1=Bonshō}}, playground equipment, and the following precinct shrines. === National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === ; Main sanctuary : Collapsed in the Great Kantō Earthquake and rebuilt in Shōwa 4. Most major timbers are reused from the pre-collapse main sanctuary rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Shōhō|Q1146192|WD=Q1146192|1=Shōhō}} 4 (1647). Registered as a National Registered Tangible Cultural Property on February 25, 2016. ; Worship hall : Rebuilt in Keiō 1 (1865). Like the main sanctuary, registered as a National Registered Tangible Cultural Property on February 25, 2016. === Important Cultural Property of Kanagawa Prefecture === ; Bell tower : In {{ill|lt=Shitoku|Q1146202|WD=Q1146202|lt=Shitoku|1=Shitoku}} 3 (1386) December, Taira Hidenori donated a bronze bell. The bell is designated an Important Cultural Property of Kanagawa Prefecture. Images of the worship hall === ファイル:Tesshu takabeya 00.jpg|Under the eaves at the front of the worship hall is a shrine-name plaque “髙部屋神社” written by {{ill|lt=Yamaoka Tesshū|Q1158894|WD=Q1158894|1=Yamaoka Tesshū}}. ファイル:Takabeya_honden.jpg|Carving on the worship-hall kōhai === Access === * About 20 minutes on foot from {{ill|lt=Isehara Station|Q4205219|WD=Q4205219|1=Isehara Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Odakyu Odawara Line|Q135176|WD=Q135176|1=Odakyū Odawara Line}}. * Bus from the station north exit on {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Chūō Kōtsū|Q10854960|WD=Q10854960|1=Kanagawa Chuo Kotsu}}: ** {{ill|lt=I76 route|Q11588882|WD=Q11588882|1=I76 route}} bound for {{ill|lt=Aiko-Ishida Station|Q1107009|WD=Q1107009|1=Aikō-Ishida Station}}, get off at “Kasuya-Kamijuku,” 3-minute walk. ** {{ill|lt=I74 route|Q11588882|WD=Q11588882|1=I74 route}} bound for Aiko-Ishida Station, get off at “Awakubo Iriguchi,” 4-minute walk. === Footnotes === content == {{神社 |名称 = Takabeya Shrine |画像 = [[File:高部屋神社.jpg|250px]]
Torii and worship hall |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513|WD=Q127513|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Isehara|Q734520|WD=Q734520|1=Isehara, Kanagawa}} Shimokasuya 2202 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = Kamuyamatoiwarehiko-no-Mikoto, Hondawake-no-Mikoto, Mitsutsuo-no-Mikoto, Ōsasagi-no-Mikoto, Okinagatarashihime-no-Mikoto, Iwanohime-no-Mikoto |社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (minor), {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} (former {{ill|lt=modern shrine rank system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=shrine rank|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}) |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = |別名 = Hachiman Shrine (Kasuya Hachiman) |例祭 = {{ill|lt=September 15|Q2846|WD=Q2846|1=September 15}} |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Kanagawa|label=Takabeya Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Takabeya Shrine''' (たかべやじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Shimokasuya, {{ill|lt=Isehara|Q734520|WD=Q734520|1=Isehara, Kanagawa}}, {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|Q127513|WD=Q127513|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}}. It is considered one of the 13 Engishiki shrines (minor shrine) in {{ill|lt=Sagami Province|Q1053732|WD=Q1053732|1=Sagami Province}} listed in the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}' of Enchō 5 ({{ill|lt=927|Q30470|WD=Q30470|1=927}}){{sfn|新編相模国風土記稿 下糟屋村 八幡宮}}, though as noted below there are also competing claim shrines ({{ill|lt=ronsha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}}). Its former shrine rank was Village Shrine. === Overview === It was formerly the village shrine of {{ill|lt=Takabeya Village|Q11672972|WD=Q11672972|1=Takabeya}} in {{ill|lt=Ōsumi District|Q11432367|WD=Q11432367|1=Ōsumi district}} with the same shrine name, and was also revered as the general tutelary shrine of {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kasuya-shō|1=UNKNOWN}}The place name Kasuya of Kasuya-shō still remains, but note that “Kami'''Kasu'''ya” and “Shimo'''Kasu'''ya” are written with different characters., including this district. Its founding date is unknown. It is listed in the '{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=UNKNOWN}}' as one of 13 seats in Sagami Province and was the general tutelary shrine of 127 villages in Ōsumi District. However, {{ill|lt=Takamori Shrine (Isehara)|Q0|WD=Q0|lt=Takamori Shrine|1=Takamori Shrine}} in Takamori, Isehara, is also considered one of the claim shrines for the Shikinaisha “Takabeya Shrine.” One reason is that this shrine is said to have been called “Takabeya Shrine” before the Meiji-era {{ill|lt=separation of Shinto and Buddhism order|Q1970535|WD=Q1970535|1=Shinbutsu bunri}}, and inscriptions reading “Takabeya Shrine” remain on timbers and on the back side of the stone monument beside its {{ill|lt=torii|Q234731|WD=Q234731|1=Torii}}[http://w1.oroti.net/~takamori/htdocs/ Sagami no Kuni Takamori Shrine] (official website), history page.. In the {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205|1=Kamakura period}}, Kasuya Tōta Saemon no Jō Arisue ({{ill|lt=Kasuya Arisue|Q11604765|WD=Q11604765|1=Kasuya Arisue}}), a retainer of {{ill|lt=Minamoto no Yoritomo|Q242800|WD=Q242800|1=Minamoto no Yoritomo}} and local {{ill|lt=jitō|Q1329805|WD=Q1329805|1=Jitō}} of Kasuya-shō, built a new shrine building with Takabeya Shrine as his guardian deity. A bronze bell made in Shitoku 3 ({{ill|lt=1386|Q6355|WD=Q6355|1=1386}}) December by Munekuni, governor of Kawachi, and donated by Taira Hidenori is designated an Important Cultural Property of Kanagawa Prefecture. In Tenbun 20 ({{ill|lt=1551|Q6560|WD=Q6560|1=1551}}), local jitō Watanabe Iwami-no-kami restored the shrine buildings{{sfn|新編相模国風土記稿 下糟屋村 八幡宮}}. In Tenshō 19 ({{ill|lt=1591|Q6766|WD=Q6766|1=1591}}) November, it received a red-seal grant of 10 koku of shrine land from {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|Q171977|WD=Q171977|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}}{{sfn|新編相模国風土記稿 下糟屋村 八幡宮}}. Until around the mid-Edo period, it was also called “Kasuya Hachimangū.” The main sanctuary was rebuilt in Shōhō 4 ({{ill|lt=1647|Q6935|WD=Q6935|1=1647}}), but collapsed in the {{ill|lt=Great Kantō Earthquake|Q3742341|WD=Q3742341|1=aftermath of 1923 Great Kantō earthquake}}; in Shōwa 4 ({{ill|lt=1929|Q18792|WD=Q18792|1=1929}}) it was rebuilt reusing the columns, carvings, and front doors. The worship hall was rebuilt in Keiō 1 ({{ill|lt=1865|Q7708|WD=Q7708|1=1865}}). The shrine preserves three old gagaku masks used in rituals from the Kamakura period onward, and a plaque reading “Hachimangū” written in early Genroku by Etsuzan Dōsō, the 7th abbot of {{ill|lt=Manpuku-ji|Q576318|WD=Q576318|1=Manpuku-ji}} (Ōbaku-zan, Uji, Kyoto), a monk who came from Jinjiang County, Quanzhou Prefecture, Fujian, China. Gyōan and Gyōhaku, who donated the komainu in front of the main sanctuary, were also monks of the {{ill|lt=Ōbaku school|Q384728|WD=Q384728|1=Ōbaku}}, suggesting a period of strong ties with Ōbaku along with the former bettō temple Jingū-ji. At present it is concurrently administered by {{ill|lt=Isehara Daijingū|Q11378955|WD=Q11378955|1=Isehara Daijingu}}, where one may also receive {{ill|lt=shuin|Q7504772|WD=Q7504772|lt=seal stamps|1=Shuin}}. === History === * {{ill|lt=1551|Q6560|WD=Q6560|1=1551}} (Tenbun 20) - Shrine buildings rebuilt by local {{ill|lt=jitō|Q1329805|WD=Q1329805|1=Jitō}} Watanabe Iwami-no-kami. * {{ill|lt=1591|Q6766|WD=Q6766|1=1591}} (Tenshō 19) - 10-koku red-seal grant donated by Tokugawa Ieyasu. * {{ill|lt=1865|Q7708|WD=Q7708|1=1865}} (Keiō 1) - Shrine buildings restored. * {{ill|lt=1923|Q2169|WD=Q2169|1=1923}} (Taishō 12) - Main sanctuary collapsed in the {{ill|lt=Great Kantō Earthquake|Q3742341|WD=Q3742341|1=aftermath of 1923 Great Kantō earthquake}}. * {{ill|lt=1929|Q18792|WD=Q18792|1=1929}} (Shōwa 4) - Main sanctuary rebuilt. === Rituals === [[File:Takabeya-jinja(Isehara) Kaguraden.jpg|200px|right|thumb|Kagura hall]] A ritual called “Shiokumi no Shinji” has been held in September for over 1,000 years since the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292|1=Heian period}}. The beach sand used in the rite is taken from Terugasaki Coast, where {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sumiyoshi Ōkami|WD=Q1114013|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin}} (Ōsumi Daimyōjin) is said to have landed. Until around the early {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} period, the shrine duty group and priest walked the 16 {{ill|lt=kilometer|Q828224|WD=Q828224|lt=km|1=Kilometre}} one way to Terugasaki to collect seawater, beach sand, and hondawara seaweed (sargassum). The seawater is used as fire-quelling sacred water; the beach sand is spread on the approach for purification; the hondawara is hung on shimenawa at the worship hall and torii. === Precincts === Within the precincts are the main sanctuary, worship hall, {{ill|lt=kagura hall|Q94498|WD=Q94498|1=Kagura-den}}, purification place, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=temizuya|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=bonshō|Q8192099|WD=Q8192099|lt=temple bell|1=Bonshō}}, playground equipment, and the following precinct shrines. === National Registered Tangible Cultural Properties === ; Main sanctuary : Collapsed in the Great Kantō Earthquake and rebuilt in Shōwa 4. Most major timbers are reused from the pre-collapse main sanctuary rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Shōhō|Q1146192|WD=Q1146192|1=Shōhō}} 4 (1647). Registered as a National Registered Tangible Cultural Property on February 25, 2016. ; Worship hall : Rebuilt in Keiō 1 (1865). Like the main sanctuary, registered as a National Registered Tangible Cultural Property on February 25, 2016. === Important Cultural Property of Kanagawa Prefecture === ; Bell tower : In {{ill|lt=Shitoku|Q1146202|WD=Q1146202|lt=Shitoku|1=Shitoku}} 3 (1386) December, Taira Hidenori donated a bronze bell. The bell is designated an Important Cultural Property of Kanagawa Prefecture. Images of the worship hall === ファイル:Tesshu takabeya 00.jpg|Under the eaves at the front of the worship hall is a shrine-name plaque “髙部屋神社” written by {{ill|lt=Yamaoka Tesshū|Q1158894|WD=Q1158894|1=Yamaoka Tesshū}}. ファイル:Takabeya_honden.jpg|Carving on the worship-hall kōhai === Access === * About 20 minutes on foot from {{ill|lt=Isehara Station|Q4205219|WD=Q4205219|1=Isehara Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Odakyu Odawara Line|Q135176|WD=Q135176|1=Odakyū Odawara Line}}. * Bus from the station north exit on {{ill|lt=Kanagawa Chūō Kōtsū|Q10854960|WD=Q10854960|1=Kanagawa Chuo Kotsu}}: ** {{ill|lt=I76 route|Q11588882|WD=Q11588882|1=I76 route}} bound for {{ill|lt=Aiko-Ishida Station|Q1107009|WD=Q1107009|1=Aikō-Ishida Station}}, get off at “Kasuya-Kamijuku,” 3-minute walk. ** {{ill|lt=I74 route|Q11588882|WD=Q11588882|1=I74 route}} bound for Aiko-Ishida Station, get off at “Awakubo Iriguchi,” 4-minute walk. === Footnotes ===
